name: Futura®100 Khmer
designer: Smich Smanloh, Sovichet Tep, Anuthin Wongsunkakon, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: A monolinear, loopless geometric sans in the tradition of Paul Renner's Futura, adapted for the Khmer script while maintaining the low-contrast geometric rationality of the original.
summary: A Khmer-script member of TypeTogether's 23-script expansion of Paul Renner's Futura, drawn after two years of archival research to honour the original's geometric rigour.
description: Part of the Futura®100 project, this Khmer cut was led by Smich Smanloh and Sovichet Tep, applying the monolinear, loopless geometry of Renner's 1927 design to a script that had never had a proper Futura companion. The family runs in six weights from Light to Bold with matching obliques and a variable font offering a weight axis, plus an optical size axis to improve readability at small sizes. It sits within a wider release targeting more than 90% of the world's population across 23 scripts, making it a credible option for multinational brands requiring consistent visual identity across writing systems.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Futura®100 Khmer as a contemporary loopless family built for today's digital tools and multiscript use, developed in concert with Bauer Types after more than two years of archival research to accurately honour Renner's original intention. They position it as not an evolution but a true expansion across time, offering six weights with obliques and an optical size axis for enhanced reading, as part of a planned 23-script release covering more than 90% of the world's population.

name: Postea Arabic
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Azza Alameddine
classification_notes: Postea Arabic is explicitly described as a geometric sans serif with calligraphic Arabic structures blended in; it belongs to the geometric sans category, adapted for Arabic script alongside a five-script family.
summary: A geometric sans-serif Arabic typeface by Azza Alameddine, designed to match the colour and rhythm of Veronika Burian and José Scaglione's wider five-script Postea family.
description: Postea Arabic blends the constructed logic of geometric type design with the calligraphic structures native to Arabic script, producing a family legible at text sizes and distinctive at display scale. Seven upright styles run from Hairline to Extrabold, with a variable font axis, and spacing is deliberately tighter in Hairline for headline use and looser in the regular weights for running text. Stylistic sets provide geometric and typographic alternates, Urdu variants, and an embedded icon system covering wayfinding, social interface and sanitary symbols, activated via a colon-bookend naming convention.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Postea Arabic as adding a century of lessons from geometric type design to Arabic's calligraphic feel, designed to match the colour and rhythm of the broader Postea family across five scripts — Arabic, Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew and Latin. They position it as ideal for branding, signage, corporate typefaces and magazines, and highlight its deliberate play between character widths, weight-specific spacing, and a full suite of stylistic sets including icons.

name: Atlante
classification_notes: Atlante draws on baroque, Garalde, transitional and modern serif traditions simultaneously, with high contrast, sharp forms and a strong italic character placing it closest to the transitional serif subcategory, though it deliberately blurs those boundaries.
summary: A high-contrast serif by Yorlmar Campos and Martín Sesto that merges flat-pen and flexible-pen traditions across 36 styles in paired display and text optical sizes.
description: Atlante draws simultaneously from baroque, Garalde, transitional and modern serif histories, shaped by the formal logic of both the flat pen and the flexible pen to produce something that sits outside any single category. The display styles push contrast and sharpness to the limit, while the text styles blunt those extremes for reliable small-size performance. An extensive suite of swashes, alternates, ligatures and stylistic sets gives the family considerable expressive range beyond what its serious-serif silhouette might suggest.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Atlante as an emotional serif for text and display, designed by Yorlmar Campos and Martín Sesto and deliberately informed by the dual writing tools of the flat pen and flexible pen. They position it as blending baroque, Garalde, transitional and modern influences into a single excessive yet conventional family, available in 36 styles or two variable fonts, with swashes, ligatures and alternates in abundance.

name: Futura®100 Myanmar
designer: Ben Mitchell, Sovichet Tep, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: A geometric monolinear sans in the tradition of Paul Renner's Futura, extended to the Myanmar script with rational, low-contrast letterforms consistent with the parent family's geometric principles.
summary: A Myanmar-script extension of the Futura®100 project, designed by Ben Mitchell and Sovichet Tep to bring Renner's geometric rationality to Burmese and Mon typography.
description: Part of TypeTogether's sweeping 23-script expansion of Paul Renner's original 1927 design, the Myanmar cut was drawn by Ben Mitchell and Sovichet Tep with six weights and obliques. The family follows the monolinear, low-contrast structure of the broader Futura®100 project, adapted for the specific stroke logic and joining conventions of Myanmar script. An optical size axis is included for improved small-text readability across digital and print contexts.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Futura®100 Myanmar as a family constructed of rational forms and balanced by slight warmth, created after more than two years of archival research to honour Renner's original intention. The Myanmar script is one of 23 planned for the full Futura®100 release, designed for multinational branding, digital devices, and modern publishing, with six weights, obliques, and an optical size axis.

name: Adelle
classification_notes: Adelle is explicitly a slab serif designed for editorial use, with bracketed serifs and a slightly dark colour that is characteristic of newspaper slab designs. Its energetic character at display sizes and neutral texture at text sizes place it firmly in the editorial slab tradition.
summary: A slab serif designed by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione for intensive editorial use in newspapers, magazines and on screen, with award recognition from the TDC and European Design Awards.
description: Conceived specifically for newspapers and magazines, Adelle delivers a neutral, dark texture at text sizes while revealing more personality at larger settings through a series of considered details. A condensed series of seven weights with matching italics extends the family for space-constrained layouts without sacrificing legibility. Cyrillic was developed with Russian designer Alexandra Korolkova and Greek with Irene Vlachou, giving all 14 extended styles genuine script-specific authority.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle as a versatile and authoritative slab serif with no shortage of personality, designed by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione for intensive editorial use in newspapers, magazines and online. They note its superior screen rendering and cross-platform consistency have made it one of their most popular webfonts, and that the condensed series shares the same personality and skeleton as the main family to create a harmonious texture when combined.

name: LFT Arnoldo
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a flared humanist sans, LFT Arnoldo combines rational contemporary proportions with glyphic, incised details drawn from stone-cut lettering traditions, placing it firmly in the humanist sans subcategory with display ambitions.
summary: A flared humanist sans designed for Oscar Mondadori's book catalogue, carrying incised, stone-cut character across seven weights from title to text use.
description: Originally conceived as an all-caps cover typeface for Oscar Mondadori, one of Italy's largest publishers, LFT Arnoldo was subsequently extended into a full humanist sans family spanning Light to Black with italic counterparts throughout. The design balances rational contemporary proportions with glyphic, chiselled details that give each letter a quietly monumental quality, making it as composed in long text as it is arresting at display sizes. A variable font is included, and the family ships with small caps, oldstyle and lining figures, alternates and ligatures.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe LFT Arnoldo as a flared humanist sans that began life as an all-caps cover typeface created during the rebranding of Oscar Mondadori, Italy's most important publisher with over 4,500 titles. The brief demanded a coherent, flexible paradigm that could work across a diverse catalogue spanning ancient classics to children's books, and the design evolved from that original branding commission into a full family with seven roman weights, matching italics, variable fonts and an extensive suite of OpenType features including small caps, multiple numeral sets and science and maths capabilities.

name: Alizé
classification_notes: Alizé is classified as a chancery italic, drawing directly from 16th-century Italian cancellaresca tradition. It has no upright roman, treating the italic as the primary style in keeping with early modern printing conventions.
summary: A three-weight chancery italic by Tom Grace, rooted in 16th-century cancellaresca with high contrast, a sharp slope and deliberate handmade irregularities.
description: Designed by Tom Grace and named after the easterly trade winds that blow from Africa to the Caribbean, Alizé takes the Venetian-style chancery italic as its departure point and pushes its low x-height and long ascenders further than historical precedent. There is no upright roman: the italic serves as the primary style, with capitals and figures only slightly inclined to echo the typographic conventions of the 16th and 17th centuries. The result is a delicate, lively face suited to single words, display lines and short passages rather than extended body text.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Alizé as a three-weight family inspired by sweeping 16th-century chancery italics, notable for its high contrast, syncopated axes and subtle handmade irregularities. They note that it promotes the italic as the primary style in keeping with early modern printing conventions, and that its sharp slope gives a prominent sweep across the page reminiscent of its trade winds namesake.

name: Futura®100
designer: Paul Renner, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: A revival and global expansion of Paul Renner's 1927 Futura, the archetype of geometric sans-serif design, built on circular forms, near-zero stroke contrast and minimal decoration.
summary: A two-year archival revision of Paul Renner's 1927 Futura, expanded into 23 scripts and six weights to serve genuinely global branding and publishing needs.
description: Developed in partnership with Bauer Types, this release brings Futura closer to Renner's original letterform intentions while extending it far beyond the handful of scripts the typeface has historically covered. Six weights run across two optical size variants — a display cut and a Text subfamily — each with matching obliques, and a variable font axis covers weight and optical size in selected scripts. Script coverage spans Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, Arabic, Armenian, Georgian, Hebrew, Khmer, Lao, Thai, Myanmar and PanAfrican Latin, with Simplified Chinese, Hangeul and major Indic scripts planned by 2026.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Futura®100 as a historically introspective yet globally applicable expansion of Renner's geometric ideal, released after more than two years of archival research and letterform comparison. Produced in concert with Bauer Types, it will eventually cover 23 scripts with six weights each, obliques and an optical size axis, targeting agencies and brands that need one coherent geometric typeface to communicate across more than 90% of the world's population.

name: Dargon
classification_notes: Dargon is classified as display due to its primary uncial-inspired stylistic sets designed for dramatic, large-scale use in fantasy contexts, though its text subfamily functions as a serifed text face with prominent serifs and flaring stems.
summary: An uncial-inspired fantasy type family by Anne-Dauphine Borione, built across six display and five text styles for gaming UIs, novel covers and editorial use.
description: Winner of the 2023 Gerard Unger Scholarship, Dargon splits into two distinct parts: six uncial display fonts named after schools of magic from a popular RPG, and a text subfamily of five weights with italics suited to continuous reading on screen and coated paper. The uncial styles carry sharp, dramatic letterforms designed for TV-scale interfaces, game packaging and RPG promotional materials, while the text weights offer low contrast, generous spacing and a rich texture that holds up in body copy. A variable font format covers the text subfamily, and the full family supports over 200 Latin-based languages.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Dargon as carved by light, an epic type family made by and for fantasy themes, with future medieval aesthetics suited to fantastic graphic quests across screens and print. They highlight its dual structure of uncial display fonts and legible text styles, noting its design for gaming UIs, dialogue menus, novel covers, RPG magazines and game packaging, and point to its variable font format and 200-plus language support as marks of its flexibility across the full campaign of a fantasy production.

name: Adelle Sans
classification_notes: A grotesque sans serif drawing on the traditional grotesque model, with the lively character and unobtrusive quality typical of that genre. Designed explicitly as a companion to the slab serif Adelle, with weight and proportion matching across both families.
summary: A 30-style grotesque sans by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, designed to pair precisely with the slab serif Adelle across weight, proportion and an exceptionally broad range of scripts.
description: Available in eight weights with matching italics plus a condensed width in seven weights, every style is engineered to optically match its Adelle slab serif counterpart, making mixed-family setting unusually coherent. Beyond the Latin family, Adelle Sans has expanded into nine scripts covering Arabic, Armenian, Chinese, Cyrillic, Devanagari, Greek and Thai, each developed in close collaboration with script specialists. The Latin character set includes small caps, multiple figure styles and support for over 245 Latin-based languages, making it one of the more thoroughly considered grotesque families available.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans as a flexible and personable world-ready sans, designed by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione as a grotesque companion to their slab serif Adelle. They emphasise its 30 Latin styles, condensed widths, and exhaustive multilingual expansion across nine scripts, noting that each script is optically matched in proportion to the Latin and bundled with it for harmonious cross-script setting.

name: Lipa Agate
classification_notes: A two-height sans-serif designed for microtypography, with ink-trap-style construction at glyph joins to maintain legibility at 5–10pt. Its functional origins and modern, space-saving proportions place it firmly in the grotesque sans tradition.
summary: A two-height sans-serif by Ermin Međedović engineered for text as small as 5–10pt, with ink-trap construction at glyph joins to prevent blobbing on press.
description: Developed over ten years as the first part of a larger type system, Lipa Agate addresses the specific problem of ink spread at very small sizes by removing ink at glyph joins. It comes in two primary versions, High and Low, which differ in x-height and ascender proportions while keeping consistent widths, giving designers control over the rhythm and texture of dense type settings. Twenty-four styles across regular, narrow and condensed proportions, plus OpenType features including small caps, info-numerals and fractions, make it a practical workhorse for newspaper footnotes, liner notes, data tables and film poster legal text.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Lipa Agate as a space-saving solution designed for microtypography — setting text at 5–10 points — with ink-spread compensation built into the glyph construction. Named from the Slovenian word for Linden, the national tree of Slovenia, it is the first part of a larger type system and was recognised by Fontshop, Typographica and the European Design Awards in 2014.

name: Bree Thai
designer: Panuwat Usakulwattana, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: Bree Thai adopts the modern loopless style of Thai script, the closest parallel to the handwriting-influenced Latin Bree, placing it within the sans-serif category with a humanist character derived from rhythmic, upright-italic letterforms.
summary: A seven-weight loopless Thai companion to the original Bree family, designed by Panuwat Usakulwattana to match Bree Latin's handwriting-derived rhythm and upright-italic energy.
description: Developed by Cadson Demak's Panuwat Usakulwattana and released in 2021, Bree Thai extends the multiscript Bree family into Thai script using the modern loopless style, chosen for its affinity with Bree Latin's calligraphic origins. Fourteen fonts span seven weights from Thin to Extrabold, each with an oblique companion, and glyph alternates allow subtle shifts in tone. The family sits comfortably in both headline branding and paragraph text, carrying the same confident, approachable personality as its Latin counterpart.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Bree Thai as a confident and spry Thai family based on handwriting rhythm and expressiveness, designed as a companion to the original Bree by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione. The loopless Thai style was chosen as the closest parallel to Bree Latin's handwriting influence, lending fluid shapes and measured flourishes to paragraphs while remaining well-suited to branding and headline use. Glyph alternates are included for tonal variety, and the family forms part of a growing multiscript system that also covers Arabic, Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.

name: Bree Devanagari
classification_notes: A calligraphic-influenced Devanagari design rooted in handwriting rhythm, mirroring the humanist warmth of the Latin Bree; the script's geometry and the penmanship-driven approach place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A seven-weight Devanagari companion to Bree, designed by Pooja Saxena, that balances calligraphic rhythm with the warmth of handwriting for both display and text use.
description: Pooja Saxena's Devanagari extension of the Bree family carries the original's handwriting-inflected personality into India's most widely used script, covering Hindi and Marathi with regional letter variants and over one hundred and fifty alternate forms for five base consonants. The range runs from a scant Thin to a full-bodied Extrabold, making it versatile across logos, packaging, newspaper headlines and mobile interfaces. Closed shapes such as the ikar and iikar matras echo the pen strokes visible in the Latin Bree's lowercase g, k, y and z, binding the two scripts visually.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Bree Devanagari as a fresh, friendly typographic expression in India's most widely-used script, led by Pooja Saxena's design and concept work. They highlight its sunny disposition for branding and headlines, its compact proportions suited to mobile apps and newspapers, and the extensive set of stylistic alternates that allow the family to shift from spirited to restrained across Hindi and Marathi. The font received a special mention at the 2019 GRANSHAN competition for multiscript type design.

name: Fino
classification_notes: Fino is explicitly described as a Rational (Modern) display serif with high contrast between thick and thin strokes, narrow proportions and sharp details — the defining characteristics of the Didone classification.
summary: A high-contrast Didone titling family by Ermin Međedović that extends the fashion-serif genre into a three-part system with sans and stencil counterparts.
description: Fino operates across two optical sizes per weight, with the Title cuts carrying the finest hairlines for large-scale settings and the standard cuts suited to 24–48pt ranges. Alternate uppercase letterforms swap narrow ovals for full circular shapes, and a suite of ligatures adds further expressive range. The family runs to 24 styles shared across Fino, Fino Sans and Fino Stencil, each offering six weights with italics and broad Latin script coverage.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Fino as a dramatic, contemporary Didone titling font family that amplifies the genre's most theatrical aspects while bringing uncommon flexibility to any type palette. Designed by Ermin Međedović, it is a Rational display serif with sharp details and fairly narrow proportions, available in three acts — Fino, Fino Sans and Fino Stencil — each in six weights with italics and two optical sizes, optimised for varied screen and editorial use.

name: Futura®100 Greek
designer: Paul Renner, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Kostas Bartsokas
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif directly derived from Paul Renner's 1927 Futura, retaining its low-contrast, pure geometric construction while expanding into contemporary optical size and weight axes.
summary: A meticulously researched Greek companion to TypeTogether's Futura®100, drawn by Kostas Bartsokas with consultation from Gerry Leonidas, covering 24 styles across six weights with obliques and an optical size axis.
description: Part of TypeTogether's sweeping 23-script expansion of Paul Renner's 1927 Futura, the Greek cut was drawn by Kostas Bartsokas with consultation from type scholar Gerry Leonidas. The family spans six weights from Light to Bold, each with a matching oblique, and offers separate Text optical sizes with adjusted spacing and extender lengths for smaller settings. Variable font files covering weight and optical size axes are included alongside the static styles.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Futura®100 Greek as part of a landmark expansion of Renner's original geometric design, rebuilt after more than two years of archival research to be more faithful, more readable, and fit for digital publishing. The Greek script joins 22 others in a project that aims to give Futura genuine global reach, with six weights, obliques, optical size variations, and script-specific typographic features including case-sensitive punctuation, small caps and multiple figure sets.

name: Tablet Gothic
classification_notes: A grotesque sans serif rooted in 19th-century British and German tradition, engineered for editorial use across multiple widths and weights with an almost-vertical axis.
summary: An 84-style grotesque by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, spanning six widths and seven weights with obliques, built for publications running across digital and print simultaneously.
description: Designed at TypeTogether for dense editorial environments, Tablet Gothic addresses common grotesque shortcomings such as unresolved terminals and poor internal spacing by using diagonal terminals that push the reader's eye through lines of text. Six width series from Compressed to Wide cover headline through body copy needs, and the family is also available as a variable font. Awards from Typographica, the European Design Awards and the Tokyo Type Directors Club recognised its release in 2012.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Tablet Gothic as engineered by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione for publications released simultaneously in digital and print. They characterise it as a grotesque grounded in 19th-century Britain and Germany that allows personality to stand out on the page, noting its almost-vertical axis as key to maintaining a signature look across 84 styles. The family covers over 200 Latin extended languages and is available as a variable font or as static OpenType styles.

name: Adelle Sans Extended
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a grotesque sans serif, with lively character and unobtrusive appearance typical of the grotesque genre. The design draws on traditional grotesque conventions while extending coverage to Vietnamese and Pan-African Latin scripts.
summary: A grotesque sans by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione extended specifically to cover Vietnamese and Pan-African Latin-based languages across 14 styles.
description: Running across seven weights with matching italics, the family was built with input from African type consultant Dr Don Osborn, whose research informed a character set that handles floating accents, IPA glyphs, Greek-derived shapes and connected diacritic marks across 400-plus languages. The 1,650-character set includes small caps, multiple figure sets and 35 icons designed for digital publications. Optical proportions are matched across the Latin, Vietnamese and Pan-African styles so that all three can coexist in the same block of text without disruption.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans Extended as a clean, spirited take on the traditional grotesque built for a major part of the global market, covering Vietnamese, Latin and Pan-African Latin-based languages. Designed by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione with language consultancy from Dr Don Osborn, the family offers 14 styles and over 1,650 characters, including typographic niceties such as small caps, several figure sets and icons for electronic publications. TypeTogether offer free licences to educational institutions and NGOs working in Africa or conducting African studies research.

name: LFT Etica
classification_notes: LFT Etica sits between grotesque and humanist sans, sharing the colour and forcefulness of cold grotesques but tempered by soft strokes, open counters and warm terminals. TypeTogether describe it as neither fully humanist nor fully grotesque.
summary: A 40-style grotesque family by Milan studio Leftloft and Octavio Pardo, occupying the space between cold grotesque rigour and humanist warmth.
description: Developed by Leftloft with extended styles by Octavio Pardo, LFT Etica takes the colour and forcefulness of common grotesque sans faces and softens them through open counters, gentle terminals and measured stroke contrast. The family spans regular, display, condensed and compressed widths, totalling 40 styles, and includes four numeral sets, fractions, arrows and dingbats via OpenType. It has found particular traction in web design and editorial contexts where information density demands a legible, even-tempered voice.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe LFT Etica as a moralist type family designed by Leftloft to take the force and versatility of cold grotesque faces and give them a much warmer feel. Sitting aesthetically between grotesque and humanist sans, it combines forcefulness and delicacy with sober charm. The family was later expanded with 24 additional condensed and compressed styles developed with Octavio Pardo in direct response to demand from users who had made it a go-to choice for web design.

name: Abril
classification_notes: The Display subfamily draws from classic Didone high-contrast models, while the Text subfamily takes inspiration from 19th-century slab serifs and Scotch Roman typefaces — two distinct historical lineages united under one editorial family.
summary: A 22-style editorial family by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, pairing high-contrast Didone display styles with Scotch Roman-influenced text cuts engineered for continuous reading in print and digital.
description: Abril divides into two complementary subfamilies: the Display styles, rooted in Didone models, carry strong page presence and typographic niceties including ornaments, borders and alternate figures; the Text styles draw instead from 19th-century slab serifs and Scotch Roman sources, delivering lower contrast and darker colour suited to long reading. Space economy places Abril Text alongside newspaper classics like Utopia and Nimrod, though with a more contemporary disposition and a full set of small caps. The family covers over 50 languages and has been applied across leading editorial titles and websites since its release in 2011.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Abril as a credible, contemporary interpretation of a classic newsface, created specifically for intensive editorial use in newspapers, magazines and digital media. The Display styles offer a strong, newsy presence on the page, while the Text styles were engineered from scratch for the right colour, texture and width for comfortable continuous reading, with Abril Text competing on space economy with newspaper classics such as Utopia and Nimrod.

name: Abril Titling
classification_notes: Abril Titling is a low-contrast display serif in the Scotch Roman tradition, engineered specifically for headline use with modified terminals and weight distribution optimised for tight setting across four widths.
summary: A 32-font headline family by José Scaglione and Veronika Burian, extending the award-winning Abril family with low contrast and four widths for tight, impactful news typography.
description: Developed three years after the original Abril, this titling extension addresses a specific request from the editorial design community for a low-contrast headline companion. Modified terminals, redistributed weight and reworked internal spacing allow characters to sit closer together without losing legibility or the Scotch Roman character of the original. Four widths from regular to condensed, each in four weights with matching italics, give designers 32 styles capable of covering everything from small callouts to full-bleed cover headlines.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Abril Titling as a low contrast font family for trustworthy, impactful headlines, engineered in response to demand from the editorial design community. They note that bringing characters closer together required more than adjusted tracking — it demanded modified terminals, changed weight distribution and retooled glyph shapes. The family spans four widths and four weights each with italics, totalling 32 fonts, and is optimised for both print and screen headline use.

name: Adelle Cyrillic
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Alexandra Korolkova
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a slab serif designed for intensive editorial use, with the energetic character typical of the slab genre becoming most apparent at display sizes.
summary: A Cyrillic extension of the award-winning Adelle slab serif, drawn for intensive editorial use in print and on screen.
description: Originally conceived by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione for newspapers and magazines, this Cyrillic expansion was developed in consultation with Russian designer Alexandra Korolkova to ensure typographic integrity across scripts. Fourteen styles span Thin to Heavy with matching italics, offering a neutral texture at text sizes that opens into a more characterful voice at headline scale. Its screen rendering and cross-platform consistency have made it one of TypeTogether's most widely used webfonts.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Cyrillic as a versatile and authoritative slab serif with no shortage of personality, designed specifically for intensive editorial use in newspapers, magazines and online contexts. Russian designer Alexandra Korolkova consulted on the Cyrillic script to ensure typographic adherence to current trends, and the family pairs with Adelle Sans, which spans twelve scripts, to form a complete editorial system.

name: Adelle Greek
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Irene Vlachou
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a slab serif throughout, conceived for intensive editorial use in newspapers and magazines, with a sturdy structure and energetic character typical of the genre.
summary: A slab serif designed for intensive editorial use, extended to Greek script by specialist Irene Vlachou across 14 styles with Cyrillic support also included.
description: Originally conceived by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione for newspapers, magazines and the web, this Greek extension brings the family's characteristic slightly dark colour and excellent texture to monotonic Greek. The 14 styles run from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, and a condensed series of seven weights offers space-saving options without sacrificing legibility. Greek expert Irene Vlachou designed the Greek script to ensure typographic adherence to current conventions, giving the extension genuine authority.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Greek as a versatile and authoritative slab serif with no shortage of personality, designed for intensive editorial use in newspapers, magazines and online. All 14 styles support Cyrillic and monotonic Greek, with Greek script designed by Irene Vlachou and Cyrillic consulted on by Alexandra Korolkova. Its intermediate weights deliver a neutral text-setting look, while larger sizes reveal the energetic character inherent to the slab serif genre.

name: Adelle Mono
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Irene Vlachou
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface with slab serif characteristics inherited from the Adelle family, offering both strict monospaced and proportional widths within the same family.
summary: A two-width slab monospace family with a true monospaced cut for developers and a proportional Flex cut for general reading, connected by a variable font spanning both.
description: Designed by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione with Irene Vlachou, the family extends the Adelle slab serif lineage into monospaced and proportional territory across 20 styles. The Mono cut holds to strict character-width constraints for coding environments like Sublime and Terminal, while Flex relaxes those constraints for branding, UI, reports and continuous reading. A variable font with weight and width axes lets the two ends of the spectrum work together within a single file, making it practical to mix contexts without switching families.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Mono as a flexible two-width family designed with both the developer and the aesthete in mind. The Mono width serves coding and programming needs with strict character alignment, while Adelle Mono Flex provides a proportional version suited to branding, UI, posters and paragraphs. A variable font spans both widths via a spectrum axis alongside a weight axis, and the family is intended to complement the existing Adelle and Adelle Sans families.

name: Adelle Sans Arabic
designer: Azza Alameddine, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: An Arabic extension of the Adelle Sans grotesque sans-serif family, maintaining the organic, flowing qualities of Arabic script while matching the grotesque tone and proportions of the Latin original.
summary: Azza Alameddine's Arabic extension of the Adelle Sans family, offering eight weights that match the grotesque tone of the Latin across editorial, branding and advertising contexts.
description: Designed by Azza Alameddine as a companion to Veronika Burian and José Scaglione's award-winning Adelle Sans, the Arabic cuts run from Ultra Thin to Heavy and are engineered to optically match the vertical proportions of the Latin. Arabic script's inherent organic quality is accommodated by softening the rigidity of the grotesque model, preserving connected flowing strokes while keeping colour and weight consistent across scripts. The broader Adelle Sans family spans Latin, Greek, Cyrillic, Armenian and Arabic, making it a practical choice for multilingual publishing and multicultural branding work.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans Arabic as an inherently organic Arabic sans that is flexible and personable in all text environments, designed as a translation of the Latin's forms into the voice of some 230 million Arabic speakers. They emphasise that adding Arabic support is not a matter of matching geometry stroke for stroke, but of ensuring each script maintains its own identity while harmonising with the others across a world-ready multilingual family.

name: Adelle Sans Armenian
classification_notes: Low-contrast, clean grotesque sans with lively character and unobtrusive appearance, extended to the Armenian script while maintaining the proportions and tone of the Latin original.
summary: A grotesque sans extension of the award-winning Adelle Sans, designed to bring consistent weight, proportion and voice to Armenian and Latin in the same text block.
description: Drawn by Veronika Burian and Gor Jihanian, the family ships 14 styles spanning Thin to Heavy with matching italics, each weight calibrated to match its Latin counterpart in vertical proportions and tone. The naturally clean shapes of Adelle Sans translate well to Armenian's architectural letterforms, the result of careful research and consultation with Hrant Papazian. Part of a broader multiscript programme covering Arabic, Chinese, Devanagari, Georgian, Greek, Hebrew, Lao and Thai, with total language support across the family exceeding 400 languages.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans Armenian as a focused extension of their multi-award winning Adelle Sans family, offering a clean and spirited interpretation of traditional grotesque sans for Armenian audiences. They cite broad language support as central to modern type design, with the Armenian script updated by Gor Jihanian as part of TypeTogether's ongoing multilingual efforts across Arabic, Chinese, Cyrillic, Devanagari, Georgian, Greek, Hebrew, Lao, Latin and Thai.

name: Adelle Sans Chinese
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque sans, pairing the Hei-style Chinese typeface Lanting Hei with the Latin Adelle Sans in a multiscript family. Both scripts share low contrast and a clean, open character consistent with the grotesque tradition.
summary: A collaboration between TypeTogether and FounderType that pairs seven weights of Adelle Sans with the Lanting Hei Chinese family for genuinely balanced bilingual layouts.
description: Bundling FounderType's Lanting Hei with seven corresponding Adelle Sans weights, this family treats Chinese and Latin characters as equals rather than an afterthought, with particular attention paid to shared punctuation. The Hei style brings a modern, highly legible take on Chinese grotesque tradition, while Adelle Sans provides the Latin counterpart in seven weights from Ultrathin to Extrabold. Suited to editorial, branding, signage and cross-media platforms where Latin and Chinese must coexist without compromise.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans Chinese as their first official FounderType global collaboration, harmonising the Lanting Hei family with Adelle Sans to create a world-ready multiscript family. They emphasise that Chinese and Latin characters are treated as equals, with careful attention to punctuation so both scripts support one another, and position the release as the starting point for broader Chinese enterprise internationalisation.

name: Adelle Sans Cyrillic
designer: Veronika Burian, Vera Estafieva, Octavio Pardo, José Scaglione
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque sans serif with low contrast and clean, spirited shapes typical of the grotesque tradition.
summary: A Cyrillic extension of the award-winning Adelle Sans family, designed to match its Latin counterpart precisely in weight and vertical proportions across 16 styles.
description: Drawn by Veronika Burian with Vera Estafieva and Octavio Pardo, this Cyrillic cut extends Adelle Sans into a clean, low-contrast grotesque with cultural sensitivity and typographic consistency at its core. Each of the eight weights has a matching italic, and every style aligns optically with its Latin, Arabic, Greek and other multiscript siblings across the broader Adelle Sans family. The result is a dependable tool for multilingual editorial, branding and signage work where script coherence across a long document matters.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans Cyrillic as a flexible and personable extension of their multi-award-winning Adelle Sans family, offering 16 styles that match the Latin in weight and vertical proportions. They cite careful research and close collaboration to ensure typographic consistency, legibility and cultural awareness, and position the Cyrillic release as part of their ongoing commitment to multilingual type design across more than 400 languages.

name: Adelle Sans Devanagari
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Erin McLaughlin, Pooja Saxena, Vaibhav Singh
classification_notes: A low-contrast grotesque sans serif extended into Devanagari script, maintaining the clean, spirited character of the original Latin Adelle Sans while adapting to Devanagari's rounder, more complex letterforms and vertical metrics.
summary: A grotesque sans extended into Devanagari script by a team of five designers over five years, covering over 400 million speakers of Hindi and related languages.
description: Rooted in Veronika Burian and José Scaglione's award-winning Adelle Sans, the Devanagari extension involved Erin McLaughlin, Vaibhav Singh and Pooja Saxena before its release with a Granshan award in 2019. Seven weights from Thin to Heavy are optically calibrated to match the Latin's proportions and colour, accommodating Devanagari's deeper vertical metrics, headline bar and rounder strokes. The family sits within TypeTogether's broader multilingual programme alongside Arabic, Armenian, Cyrillic and Greek counterparts, each engineered to harmonise across scripts in the same document.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans Devanagari as an organic, low-contrast Devanagari font family that translates the Latin's forms into the voice of over 400 million people. They emphasise that matching scripts requires more than geometric correspondence — each must maintain its own identity while harmonising with the others — and position this release as part of their ongoing commitment to broad multilingual type support.

name: Adelle Sans Georgian
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Jan Charvát, Akaki Radmadze
classification_notes: A grotesque sans serif rooted in the award-winning Adelle Sans Latin family, the Georgian extension adapts the clean, spirited character of the original while accommodating the more organic, curve-led nature of the Georgian script.
summary: A Georgian extension of the award-winning Adelle Sans, designed to harmonise with the Latin across 14 weights while respecting the organic, curve-led nature of the Georgian script.
description: Jan Charvát and Akaki Radmadze created the Georgian under the direction of Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, matching the Latin in tone, vertical proportions and weight distribution from Thin to Heavy. Georgian script's inherently flowing strokes required a softer interpretation than the Latin, yet the two sit comfortably together in mixed-script text. The family sits within a broader multilingual effort from TypeTogether that spans Arabic, Armenian, Chinese, Cyrillic, Devanagari, Greek, Hebrew, Lao, Thai and Latin Extended.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans Georgian as a flexible, personable world-ready typeface providing true multilingual support for Georgian and Latin. The Georgian design was created in the same spirit as the full Adelle Sans family, with weights from Thin to Heavy engineered to optically match Latin proportions. The release forms part of TypeTogether's broader commitment to multilingual type design, spanning over a dozen scripts, each harmonised while retaining its own cultural identity.

name: Adelle Sans Greek
designer: José Scaglione, Veronika Burian, Irene Vlachou
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque sans serif, designed as the sans counterpart to the slab serif Adelle, with weight and proportional matching across both families.
summary: A Greek-script extension of Adelle Sans, designed by Greek type specialist Irene Vlachou to sit harmoniously alongside the award-winning Adelle slab serif family.
description: Available in 14 styles across seven weights with matching italics, Adelle Sans Greek maintains the weight and vertical proportions of its slab serif companion, Adelle, allowing the two families to be mixed within the same text block without disruption. The Greek script was handled by Irene Vlachou, lending the extension genuine expertise rather than adaptation. It suits branding, signage and advertising work where a clean, lively grotesque with credible Greek support is needed.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans Greek as a clean and spirited take on the traditional grotesque sans, built for flexibility across branding, signage and advertising. Each of its 16 styles is matched in weight and vertical proportions to the slab serif Adelle, ensuring a harmonious fit and pleasing textural contrast when the two families are used together. The Greek script was designed by Greek type expert Irene Vlachou to maintain coherence across the wider Adelle Sans family.

name: Adelle Sans Hebrew
classification_notes: A Hebrew extension of the grotesque sans Adelle Sans, characterised by low contrast, organic character, and the squared forms typical of Hebrew script adapted into a grotesque sans idiom.
summary: Liron Lavi Turkenich's Hebrew translation of Adelle Sans brings the original grotesque's lively character into a seven-weight family engineered to sit harmoniously alongside its Latin, Arabic and Cyrillic counterparts.
description: Drawn to match the proportions, colour and tone of José Scaglione and Veronika Burian's award-winning Latin, the Hebrew cuts run from Thin to Heavy and are designed for demanding editorial, branding and signage contexts. Hebrew script's inherently squarer geometry, with its mix of rounded corners and discrete straight strokes, is handled with care so that each weight reads as a genuine companion to the Latin rather than a retrofit. The family sits within TypeTogether's broader multilingual effort spanning Arabic, Armenian, Chinese, Cyrillic, Devanagari, Greek, Georgian, Lao, Thai and more.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans Hebrew as an organic, low-contrast Hebrew font family that is flexible and personable in global text environments, designed by Liron Lavi Turkenich as a translation of the original Adelle Sans into Hebrew script. They note that adding language support requires each script to harmonise with the others while maintaining its own identity, and position the release as part of their ongoing multilingual effort across more than a dozen scripts.

name: Adelle Sans Korean
designer: Chorong Kim, Changseop Im, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: Adelle Sans Korean is a Hangul extension of the original Adelle Sans grotesque, maintaining the clean, unobtrusive character of the grotesque tradition while adapting to the structural demands of Korean script.
summary: A Hangul extension of the Adelle Sans grotesque family, designed in partnership with Sandoll to match the weight, texture and spirit of its Latin counterpart across seven weights.
description: Designed by Chorong Kim and Changseop Im of Sandoll alongside Veronika Burian and José Scaglione's original Latin, this Korean companion to Adelle Sans runs from Ultrathin to Extrabold with a focus on legibility at small sizes and space-saving forms suited to Korean script's dense, combined marks. The design sits within a broader multilingual system that spans Latin, Arabic, Armenian, Chinese, Cyrillic, Devanagari, Georgian, Greek, Hebrew, Lao and Thai, each script optically calibrated to match the proportions of the others. Each font includes the full Latin character set alongside the Korean, making it a practical tool for multicultural branding and editorial work in mixed-language environments.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans Korean as a flexible and personable Korean font family designed for demanding editorial and screen environments. Created in partnership with Sandoll, they highlight its space-saving design, improved legibility at small sizes, modern appeal and harmonious texture relative to the wider Adelle Sans family. They position it as part of their ongoing multilingual expansion, noting that each script in the family is engineered to optically match its counterparts while maintaining its own cultural identity.

name: Adelle Sans Lao
designer: Suppakit Chalermlarp, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as an organic, low-contrast grotesque sans, extending the Adelle Sans grotesque family into Lao script with matching texture and weight range.
summary: A Lao extension of the award-winning Adelle Sans grotesque, drawn by Suppakit Chalermlarp to match the original family's weight, texture and editorial versatility across seven weights.
description: Designed to sit alongside Veronika Burian and José Scaglione's Latin grotesque, the Lao cuts span Thin to Heavy and are engineered to optically match the proportions of their Latin counterparts. The design addresses Lao script's particular complexity, including dense combined marks and loanword conventions, while preserving the clean, low-contrast character of the broader Adelle Sans family. In addition to Lao, each weight includes the full Latin character set, making it a practical tool for multilingual editorial, branding and signage work.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans Lao as an organic, low-contrast Lao font family built for demanding editorial design problems. Created by Suppakit Chalermlarp in the spirit of the original Adelle Sans, it offers seven weights from Thin to Heavy and forms part of the wider Adelle Sans multilingual family, which spans Arabic, Armenian, Chinese, Cyrillic, Devanagari, Greek, Georgian, Hebrew, Latin, Latin Extended, Thai and Lao. The foundry emphasise that each script has been engineered to optically match its counterparts rather than simply duplicating geometry stroke for stroke.

name: Adelle Sans Thai
designer: Smich Smanloh, Anuthin Wongsunkakon, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: A loopless Thai extension of the Adelle Sans grotesque family, sharing the clean low-contrast forms and lively character of its Latin counterpart while adhering to Thai script conventions.
summary: A loopless Thai extension of the award-winning Adelle Sans grotesque, engineered to optically match its Latin, Arabic, Armenian, Cyrillic, Devanagari and Greek counterparts across seven weights.
description: Designed by Smich Smanloh and Anuthin Wongsunkakon to sit alongside Veronika Burian and José Scaglione's original Latin, the Thai draws on loopless forms — a modern simplification of Thai handwriting — to share Adelle Sans's texture and colour without compromising the script's own identity. Seven weights from Thin to Heavy give editors and brand designers a full range of emphasis, while the space-saving construction improves legibility at small sizes and leaves room for Thai tonal marks. The family sits within TypeTogether's broader multilingual programme, where each script is harmonised optically rather than matched stroke for stroke.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Adelle Sans Thai as a loopless Thai font family that is flexible and personable in all text environments including screens. They note that the loopless design reflects a modern, simplified form of Thai handwriting drawn in the same spirit as the wider Adelle Sans family, and that across the full multilingual family each script has been engineered to optically match the proportions of its counterparts rather than simply mimicking their geometry.

name: Aeroplan
classification_notes: Aeroplan is a text serif with classical proportions, moderate stroke contrast, teardrop serifs and sharp wedge details, placing it within the transitional tradition while drawing on a 1916 source with distinctly contemporary refinement.
summary: A text serif by Nina Faulhaber, interpreted from a 1916 aircraft-engine book source, with teardrop serifs, sharp wedge details and a distinctly expressive italic.
description: Faulhaber, trained in sculpting, brought a tactile sensibility to this revival of a forgotten custom typeface found in a 1916 book on aircraft engines. The family runs across four weights from Regular to Bold, each with a matching italic that is characterful enough to set continuous text on its own. Details such as bracketed E and F, an atypical K connection and inktraps for print rendering position it as a genuine alternative to Baskerville in editorial, architecture and engineering publications.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Aeroplan as the first typeface by designer and sculptor Nina Faulhaber, who took a forgotten 1916 custom font as her source and reworked it for contemporary print and screen use. They highlight its teardrop serifs, sharp wedges, sturdy contrast and one of the most distinctive italics in any text serif, recommending it for magazines, books, newspapers and edgy or obscure subjects anywhere Baskerville might otherwise be used.

name: Alverata
classification_notes: Alverata draws directly from Romanesque capital inscriptions of 11th and 12th century Europe, producing short thorn-like serifs and flared forms that place it outside conventional classical or transitional serif categories. The Irregular subfamily's exchange of curves and straight lines between upper and lowercase is an unusual structural experiment within the serif genre.
summary: Gerard Unger's serif family rooted in his doctoral research on Romanesque inscriptions, extended into three structurally distinct subfamilies across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.
description: Based on Dr Gerard Unger's PhD research into 11th and 12th century Romanesque type, the family uses capital inscriptions as its structural foundation, producing short thorn-like serifs and flared terminals across 24 styles. Three subfamilies serve different needs: the Regular provides a stable text workhorse, the Informal softens forms with a single-storey 'a' and calligraphic terminals, and the Irregular inverts the relationship between curves and straight lines across upper and lowercase. Cyrillic was developed with Tom Grace and Greek with Gerry Leonidas and Irene Vlachou, making the family a genuinely broad multilingual tool.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Alverata as a contemporary, eclectic family drawn from Romanesque Europe, where capital inscriptions inform its short thorn-like serifs and flared forms. They note that the Irregular style is designed for scenarios combining uniqueness with eclectic historical reference, exchanging curved and straight characteristics between upper and lowercase rather than simply varying weight. The family was developed with collaborators for Greek and Cyrillic, and all styles have been optimised for varied screen use.

name: Alverata Cyrillic
classification_notes: Rooted in Romanesque capital inscriptions from 11th and 12th century Europe, with short thorn-like serifs and flared forms; the Irregular subfamily exchanges conventional straight and curved stroke logic, making it formally distinct from any simple historical revival.
summary: A Cyrillic extension of Gerard Unger's award-winning Alverata, drawing on Romanesque capital inscriptions to produce a serif with thorn-like serifs, flared strokes and three distinct stylistic registers.
description: Designed by Gerard Unger with Tom Grace handling the Cyrillic adaptation, the family comes in three styles: Regular, Informal and Irregular. The Irregular subfamily is the most formally adventurous, deliberately exchanging the conventional roles of curves and straight lines between upper and lowercase letters. Twenty-four fonts span six weights with italics across all three styles, and the family performs across text and display settings with a large x-height and open counters.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Alverata Cyrillic as a contemporary, eclectic typeface grounded in Romanesque Europe, with capital inscriptions providing the basis for its short thorn-like serifs and flared forms. The Irregular style experiments with letterforms by swapping curves for straight lines and vice versa, making it resolutely modern. The family is optimised for digital and physical use, offering strong legibility at small sizes and visual liveliness at large ones.

name: Alverata Greek
designer: Gerard Unger, Gerry Leonidas, Irene Vlachou
classification_notes: Rooted in Romanesque capital inscriptions from 11th and 12th century Europe, with short thorn-like serifs and flared forms that place it squarely in historical serif territory, though its deliberate exchange of curves for straight lines gives it a modern, eclectic character distinct from classical revival serifs.
summary: The Greek extension of Gerard Unger's Alverata family, developed with Gerry Leonidas and Irene Vlachou, drawing on Romanesque inscription forms to produce a contemporary serif with thorn-like serifs and deliberately exchanged stroke directions.
description: Developed by Gerard Unger alongside Greek type specialists Gerry Leonidas and Irene Vlachou, Alverata Greek applies the same Romanesque capital-inscription logic as the Latin family to the Greek script, with short serifs, flared stems and a deliberate swapping of curves and straight lines between cases. Twelve styles run from Light to Black with matching italics, covering the same range as the core family. It received significant recognition on release, appearing on Fontshop's Best of 2014, Typographica's Notable Releases and winning gold at the 2015 European Design Awards.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Alverata Greek as a contemporary, eclectic typeface inspired by type from medieval Europe, with capital inscriptions forming the basis for its short, thorn-like serifs and flared forms. They note that it experiments with letterforms by exchanging straight lines for curves and vice versa, making it resolutely modern, and that it performs well at both small text sizes and large display settings.

name: Aneto
classification_notes: A high-contrast editorial serif with a companion low-contrast text cut, designed explicitly for structured editorial publishing across screen and print. The variable font axes of weight and optical size confirm its classification as a contemporary text-and-display serif system.
summary: A dual-family editorial serif by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, split into a higher-contrast display cut and a lower-contrast text cut for full-page typographic systems.
description: Aneto and Aneto Text share the same underlying skeleton but differ in stroke contrast, with Aneto handling headings, callouts and midrange structure while Aneto Text carries body copy down to captions. Both families offer five upright and five italic styles from Regular to Black, with variable font versions covering weight and optical size axes in two files instead of twenty. The family forms the third part of a trilogy alongside Catalpa and Belarius, intended as a complete editorial suite for digital magazines, newspapers and books.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Aneto as a long-awaited editorial polymath from José Scaglione and Veronika Burian, built to cover everything from midrange headings and callouts to body paragraphs and colophons. The family took over three years to complete, with its taller x-height, enlarged counters and shortened ascenders and descenders optimised for compact, readable setting. Variable font versions collapse all 20 static styles into two files using weight and optical size axes, continuing TypeTogether's variable font programme running since 2017.

name: Aneto Skyline
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Roxane Gataud
classification_notes: A high-contrast display serif with a slender, tall structure designed explicitly for headline use across three widths, placing it firmly in the display serif category with editorial vernacular.
summary: A high-contrast display serif in three widths designed by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione for the most demanding headline roles in print and digital editorial work.
description: Part of the broader Aneto family trilogy from TypeTogether, Aneto Skyline is built for front pages and above-the-fold moments where vertical presence and contrast must stop a reader cold. Its 36 static styles span six weights from ExtraLight to ExtraBold, each with matching italics, across Normal, Condensed and Compressed widths, with optional variable fonts covering the full weight and width axes. Sharp alternate serifs on C, G and S, wedge alternates on diagonal letters, and a notably carved Q in the heaviest weights give it a characterful edge beyond mere impact.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Aneto Skyline as a display of power in three widths, created specifically to set the tallest and most impactful headlines in the compressed spaces of magazines, posters and newspapers. Lead designers Veronika Burian and José Scaglione spent over three years completing the Aneto family trilogy, of which Skyline is the most concentrated subfamily, offering 36 fonts or two variable files for maximum impact with minimum file weight.

name: Archibrazo
classification_notes: Archibrazo uses sculptural wedge serifs and carved countershapes, placing it within the wedge or lapidary serif tradition. Its calligraphic origins and optical refinements across weights align it with high-contrast editorial serifs.
summary: A seven-weight wedge serif by Argentine master Rubén Fontana, drawing on calligraphic tradition and sculptural countershapes for both continuous reading and display work.
description: Designed by Rubén Fontana and engineered for readability across a full range of settings, the family moves from open, comfortable lighter weights suited to editorial text through to the compact Rotunda style, a titling cut where carved countershapes and dense forms make it well-suited to branding and poster work. The design pays homage to Juan Andralis' Buenos Aires surrealist publishing house, El Archibrazo, and shares research into readability optimisation with Fontana's earlier Chaco family. Thirteen styles include small caps, five figure sets, discretionary ligatures and a range of fleurons.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Archibrazo as a blend of calligraphy and sculpture from master type designer Rubén Fontana, conceived with effortless readability as its central concern. They note its lighter weights excel in editorial and continuous reading contexts, whilst the heavier Extrabold and Rotunda styles suit branding, signage and poster use. The family is named in homage to El Archibrazo, the influential Buenos Aires publishing house of surrealist painter and printer Juan Andralis, and emerged from the same readability research that informed Fontana's Chaco family.

name: Arlette
classification_notes: Arlette is classified as a sans serif but departs from conventional grotesque or geometric models through its gestural, curvilinear stroke construction rooted in calligraphic speed. Its painterly character places it firmly in an expressive or experimental sans subcategory.
summary: A gestural sans serif by Pilar Cano and Ferran Milan that uses curvilinear, painterly forms to push well beyond the conventions of the genre.
description: Drawn in eight weights with matching italics, the Latin family extends to a full Thai companion in six weights, making it one of the few expressive sans serifs to address both scripts with equal care. The design originated from a single fast-stroke letterform drawn in the tradition of Roger Excoffon before evolving into something independent and more experimental. OpenType features include swashes, swash ligatures, multiple numeral sets and positional forms, making it equally suited to book covers, branding and editorial work where personality is required.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Arlette as a category-expanding sans serif that boldly experiments while remaining readable at small sizes. Based initially on a stroke from a Roger Excoffon family, designers Pilar Cano and Ferran Milan departed from that reference in favour of forms built from gestural vigour. The family covers Latin and Thai, the latter featuring decorative characters derived from palm leaf manuscripts and adapted Latin numerals, alongside contextual kerning to prevent mark clashes.

name: Arlette Thai
classification_notes: A sans serif with gestural, painterly stroke qualities derived from fast calligraphic movement, placing it within the humanist sans tradition while pushing well beyond its conventions.
summary: A gestural sans serif by Pilar Cano and Ferran Milan covering Thai and Latin, with six weights and a curvilinear energy drawn from fast stroke movement.
description: Rooted loosely in a single letterform from Roger Excoffon's work before departing into its own territory, Arlette Thai builds a six-weight family for Thai alongside a full Latin counterpart with eight weights and italics. The Thai cuts include decorative characters referencing palm leaf manuscripts and numerals adapted to Thai script height, with contextual kerning to prevent mark clashes. Swashes, swash ligatures and a broad OpenType feature set make it suited to magazines, branding, book covers and packaging across both scripts.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Arlette Thai as an imaginative and gestural sans serif that redefines what a Thai font can be, with Pilar Cano and Ferran Milan basing it on the fast stroke of a single letter from a Roger Excoffon family before abandoning that starting point in favour of experimentation. The family covers six weights from hairline to bold in Thai, includes decorative characters drawn from palm leaf manuscripts, and extends OpenType capabilities to swashes, oldstyle figures and tabular numerals. It received a special mention at Granshan 2019 and was included in the Yearbook of Type IV.

name: Athelas
classification_notes: Athelas is a humanist serif with open counters, elegant curves and a roman/italic contrast that draws on the traditions of fine British book printing, with the italic showing a more angular, calligraphic character distinct from the upright.
summary: A humanist serif by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, designed for long-form reading in fine printed books and on screen, with coverage extending to Latin, Greek and Cyrillic.
description: Designed with the traditions of British book printing in mind, Athelas pairs open counters and graceful serifs in the roman with a more angular italic that introduces quiet contrast without ever becoming harsh. Its broad character set covers most Latin-based languages alongside monotonic Greek and Cyrillic, making it a credible choice for multilingual publishing projects. The family gained notable exposure through Apple's iBooks app and the OS X Mavericks system fonts, where it was optimised for screen rendering.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Athelas as an elegant book font family that prioritises the beauty of fine book printing and makes it available digitally and in print. Inspired by British literature, it was created by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, with Greek characters by Irene Vlachou and Cyrillic by Tom Grace. The design makes full use of typographic tranquility — white space in margins, between columns, lines and words — and has been optimised for screen use, with Apple adopting it for iBooks and OS X Mavericks. It won first prize at the 2008 Granshan Type Design Competition.

name: Athelas Arabic
classification_notes: Athelas Arabic is a calligraphic Arabic text typeface designed as a companion to the Athelas Latin family, sharing its classic proportions, refined contrast and weight calibrated for extended reading in books and on screen.
summary: A refined Arabic text typeface by Sahar Afshar, reworked to sit harmoniously alongside the Athelas Latin, Greek and Cyrillic families in long-form reading contexts.
description: Designed originally as a standalone Arabic face, it was thoroughly reworked by Sahar Afshar to match the weight, contrast and spacing of the existing Athelas family. Letter sizes and colour were adjusted so that Arabic and Latin sit comfortably together on the page, preserving the fluid, calligraphic quality of the script. The four-weight family carries extensive Middle Eastern language coverage and OpenType features suited to fine digital and printed books.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Athelas Arabic as a refined companion to the Athelas family, created by Iranian designer Sahar Afshar for elegant digital and printed books. Originally an independent design, it underwent a thorough reworking to align with Athelas in weight, contrast and spacing, while retaining the natural aesthetic qualities of Arabic. The complete Athelas family, including Latin, Greek and Cyrillic, has been optimised for today's varied screen uses.

name: Athelas Cyrillic
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Irene Vlachou, Tom Grace
classification_notes: A book-oriented serif with open counters, elegant curves and graceful serifs, designed for fine book printing and screen use; the more angular italic contrasts the fluid roman, placing it firmly in the humanist serif tradition.
summary: A book serif covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, designed for fine printing and optimised for screen use, notably adopted by Apple in iBooks and OS X Mavericks.
description: Designed by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, with Cyrillic by Tom Grace and monotonic Greek by Irene Vlachou, it draws inspiration from British literary traditions while respecting the cultural requirements of diacritic-heavy scripts. Open counters and graceful serifs define the roman, while the italic turns notably more angular, creating a useful internal contrast. Its adoption by Apple for iBooks and OS X Mavericks confirms its suitability for both high-quality print and demanding screen environments.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Athelas Cyrillic as an elegant font family for books, inspired by Britain's classic literature and optimised for both digital and print use. They highlight its open counters, fluid roman forms and angular italic, as well as its large character set covering Latin, monotonic Greek and Cyrillic. Its selection by Apple for the iBooks app and OS X Mavericks is cited as recognition of its broad screen optimisation.

name: Athelas Greek
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Irene Vlachou, Tom Grace
classification_notes: A book-oriented serif with open counters, elegant curves and graceful serifs, drawing on classical British book printing traditions while accommodating the structural needs of Greek and Cyrillic scripts.
summary: A multilingual book serif covering Latin, monotonic Greek and Cyrillic, designed for fine printing and screen reading alike.
description: Originally inspired by British classic literature and designed by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, this family pairs open counters and fluid roman shapes with a more angular italic. The Greek component was drawn by Irene Vlachou and the Cyrillic by Tom Grace, each respecting the cultural conventions of their script. Apple adopted the complete Athelas family for iBooks and OS X Mavericks, which speaks to its screen optimisation credentials alongside its book printing origins.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Athelas Greek as an elegant typeface for books, equally suited to screen and print. Inspired by Britain's classic literature and designed by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, it prioritises the beauty of fine book printing through open counters, graceful serifs and typographic tranquility — the considered use of white space in margins, columns, lines, words and within characters themselves. The monotonic Greek was designed by Irene Vlachou and the Cyrillic by Tom Grace. Its adoption by Apple in iBooks and OS X Mavericks is cited as recognition of its screen optimisation.

name: AwanZaman
classification_notes: Rooted in the geometric forms of Kufic script for the Arabic component, with a monolinear, geometric sans Latin designed to harmonise in text colour and proportion; classified as geometric sans across both scripts.
summary: A dual-script geometric sans family developed across three phases by Dr Mamoun Sakkal and Juliet Shen, balancing Kufic-rooted Arabic forms with a companion Latin across seven weights.
description: The Arabic component began as a commission for a Kuwaiti newspaper, pairing Awan's geometric Kufic structure with Zaman's calligraphic Naskh details, switchable via 20 OpenType stylistic sets within a single file. Juliet Shen's Latin extension was drawn to match the Arabic's monolinear stroke weight and page colour, with four stylistic sets introducing novel letterforms that echo Arabic curves without appearing contrived. There is no italic in either script; the seven-weight range from Thin to Heavy handles contextual distinction instead.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe AwanZaman as a clever, seven-weight powerhouse designed for multilingual editorials and newspapers, making extensive use of OpenType stylistic sets — 20 in the Arabic and four in the Latin — to let designers move between geometric and calligraphic forms. The family began as a newspaper commission, grew through the collaboration of Dr Mamoun Sakkal and Juliet Shen, and supports Persian and Urdu alongside Arabic and Latin. The complete family has been optimised for contemporary screen use.

name: Belarius
classification_notes: Belarius is a variable slab serif that shifts continuously between sans and slab extremes across a serif axis, with the slab end defining its primary character; the Poster subfamily extends slab serifs to exaggerated lengths for display use.
summary: A three-axis variable family by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione that moves continuously between sans and slab serif, across narrow to wide widths and light to extrabold weights, across 90 static styles and two variable fonts.
description: Designed as the middle entry in a TypeTogether trilogy alongside Catalpa and Aneto, Belarius draws on researched editorial typesetting and vintage advertising to produce a warm, restrained character suited to packaging, magazine headlines, subheads and pull-quotes. The Poster subfamily pushes slab serif bracketing to deliberate extremes for a distinctive crackly texture in large settings, while the Sans end of the axis allows the same skeleton to work at smaller reproduction sizes. OpenType features include tabular figures for tables and charts, plus rulers, patterns, endmarks and fleurons for professional text layouts.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Belarius as a three-axis variable family shifting from sans to slab serif, condensed to expanded, and light to bold, with serifs that appear and lengthen at will. They position it as a multitool for magazine, digital editorial and packaging design, noting its warm burnished quality drawn from editorial research and vintage advertising, and cite its 90 total styles as covering hierarchy needs from running text through poster headings.

name: Bely
classification_notes: Bely's classical proportions, bracketed rectangular serifs, triangular top serifs and high contrast in the display weight place it firmly in the transitional serif tradition, with the display weight pushing French high-contrast conventions to an expressive extreme.
summary: A four-weight text serif by Roxane Gataud paired with a high-contrast French-style display weight that pushes the family's logic to an expressive extreme.
description: Built on classical proportions, the text weights balance large triangular serifs at the top with thick bracketed rectangular serifs at the bottom, giving paragraphs a distinctive texture and readable rhythm. Bely Display breaks from the restraint of the text cuts with monstrous angled contrast suited to packaging, identities and headlines. Winner of a TDC Award of Excellence in 2017 and the SOTA Catalyst Award in 2016, the family covers Latin A Extended with a full suite of OpenType features including small caps, oldstyle figures and alternate characters.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Bely as a high-class throwback text family capped by a radical, expressive French-style display weight. They highlight the balance between restraint in the text weights and fearless adventurousness in Bely Display, noting that the family is Roxane Gataud's debut and was optimised for varied screen use.

name: Birdy
classification_notes: Birdy is a blackletter typeface with inline styling, directly inspired by the inline blackletter of the Gul beer label in Iceland. The foundry describes it as a contemporary take on blackletter font families.
summary: A single-weight inline blackletter drawn live in front of an audience at the London Design Festival, inspired by the Gul beer label from Iceland.
description: Created by Veronika Burian during the Graphic Design Walk at the London Design Festival, Birdy took its cue from the inline blackletter style of an Icelandic beer logo and was drawn in a single day with visitors watching the process via projector. Available as a free download, it has since appeared at the Blacklecker exhibition in Berlin, served as the first header typeface for Alphabettes, and was produced as an iron-on sticker at Typo Berlin. A single Regular weight covers the essentials for display and headline use.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Birdy as a contemporary take on blackletter font families, created during the Graphic Design Walk as part of the London Design Festival. Designer Veronika Burian was inspired by the inline blackletter of Iceland's Gul beer label, and drew the typeface live over one day while an audience followed along via projector. The font was subsequently made available for free download.

name: Bree
classification_notes: Bree is a sans serif structured as an upright italic with strong handwriting influence, including single-story 'a', cursive 'e', and fluid diagonal strokes, placing it firmly in the humanist sans category with expressive, calligraphic traits.
summary: An upright italic sans by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione that draws on handwriting rhythm to give branding and headline work a distinctive, lively character.
description: Designed with a wide stance, extra-large x-height and calligraphic details such as a single-story 'a', cursive 'e' and flourished 'Q', Bree reads as permanently in motion even when set upright. The family spans 14 styles from Thin to Extrabold with matching obliques, and is available in variable font format. Since its 2008 release it has expanded into Arabic, Cyrillic, Greek, Thai and Latin with Pinyin and Vietnamese support, making it one of the more thoroughly developed multiscript display families in independent type publishing.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Bree as a spry, multi-award-winning upright italic influenced by handwriting, with a wide character stance, large x-height and a touch of cheekiness that gives it a big personality even at small sizes.
