The family received a major update in 2019 adding new weights, refined spacing and Cyrillic and Greek scripts, followed by Arabic and Thai in 2021, and is available in standard OpenType and variable font formats.

name: Bree Serif
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a slab serif companion to the Bree sans family, retaining handwriting-derived letterforms such as single-storey 'a' and cursive 'e', placing it firmly in the humanist slab tradition rather than a geometric or mechanistic one.
summary: A humanist slab serif by José Scaglione and Veronika Burian that carries the calligraphic personality of Bree into a more text-capable companion family.
description: Designed as a serif counterpart to TypeTogether's Bree, the family retains the sans's defining quirks including the single-storey 'a', cursive 'e' and looped italic characters, giving it warmth uncommon in slab serifs. Six weights from Thin to Extrabold, each with italics, make it workable across both headline and body copy settings. Alternate letterforms for 'a', 'e', 'g', 'k' and 'y' are available in upright styles for contexts where a more neutral register is needed.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Bree Serif as an energetic and mature slab serif that maintains the original flavour of handwriting while supporting optimal editorial usage. They position it as a separate type family in its own right, even as it follows the same theme as the sans Bree, with the slabby nature of its shapes making a strong impression particularly in heavier weights. The family has been optimised for varied screen use throughout.

name: Bree Arabic
classification_notes: Based on Ruq'ah, the informal Arabic handwriting style, Bree Arabic is a humanist design with fluid, calligraphic qualities rather than a geometric or grotesque approach. Its script-influenced forms and measured flourishes place it firmly in the humanist category.
summary: An Arabic companion to the Bree family, designed by Azza Alameddine and rooted in Ruq'ah, the informal Arabic handwriting tradition.
description: Designed by Azza Alameddine as a companion to Veronika Burian and José Scaglione's original Bree, the Arabic variant draws on Ruq'ah script to mirror the Latin's handwritten warmth and upright-italic rhythm. Seven weights run from Thin to Extrabold, with glyph alternates available to modulate tone. The family has received recognition at the European Design Festival, the GRANSHAN competition and Selección Clap, and has seen real-world use across branding, editorial and digital contexts.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Bree Arabic as a spirited and rhythmic handwriting-based Arabic, suited to display use and friendly paragraph text. They note that since Bree Latin is influenced by handwriting, the Arabic counterpart was drawn from Ruq'ah, the informal handwriting style used for Arabic, giving text a lively and approachable appearance through fluid shapes and measured flourishes. The family forms part of the broader multiscript Bree release, which also covers Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Thai.

name: Bree Cyrillic
classification_notes: Bree Cyrillic is a humanist sans structured as an upright italic, its forms rooted in handwriting rhythm with cursive strokes and fluid shapes rather than geometric or grotesque conventions.
summary: A Cyrillic extension of Bree by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, built as an upright italic with handwriting rhythm and a wide, characterful stance suited to branding and headlines.
description: Bree Cyrillic carries the original Latin family's defining trait, the upright italic structure, into the Cyrillic script, with outstroke curves on diagonals and flourished, fluid shapes that give it an inherent cursiveness. An extra-large x-height and wide character stance keep it visible and lively at smaller sizes, while alternates offer a more neutral setting when needed. The family ships in variable font format alongside seven static weights, each with an oblique, and was designed from scratch to match the Latin's tone rather than adapted from it.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Bree Cyrillic as a confident and spry family based on handwriting rhythm and expressiveness, designed by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione. The Cyrillic script was drawn from scratch in 2019 as part of a major family update that also added Book weights, Vietnamese support for the Latin, and variable font versions across the entire Bree range.

name: Bree Greek
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Irene Vlachou
classification_notes: Bree Greek is a humanist sans-serif with an upright italic structure strongly influenced by handwriting, featuring cursive letterforms, fluid shapes and an expressive rhythm that place it firmly in the humanist tradition rather than geometric or grotesque.
summary: A Greek extension of the Bree family, built as an upright italic with cursive letterforms and a wide stance suited to branding and headline work.
description: Designed by Irene Vlachou to complement the Latin original by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, Bree Greek carries the family's defining upright italic structure into the Greek script. Characteristic features include a cursive epsilon, flourished diagonal outstrokes and fluid shapes, with alternates available for a more neutral appearance. Seven weights from Thin to Extrabold, each with an oblique, are available alongside variable font versions released as part of a major 2019 family update.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Bree Greek as a spry sans serif delivering a spirited look for branding and headline use. Completed by Irene Vlachou, the Greek script was designed from scratch in 2019 to parallel the Latin's tone, featuring a cursive epsilon, flourished shapes and an extra-large x-height. The family supports variable font format and sits within a broader multiscript offering that includes Latin, Cyrillic, Arabic and Thai.

name: Capitolium 2
classification_notes: A high-contrast serif newsface with roots in Roman public lettering tradition, engineered for newsprint legibility with sturdy strokes and a tall x-height; sits between classical and transitional serif conventions.
summary: A three-series serif newsface by Gerard Unger, descended from a wayfinding commission for Rome's Jubilee 2000 and re-engineered for the demands of newsprint.
description: Originally drawn in 1998 for a pilgrim wayfinding system in Rome, Capitolium was reworked by Unger into a full newsface family with sturdier shapes, a taller x-height and tighter character-per-line economy. The family divides across three optical series: Capitolium 2 for general text, Capitolium News 2 for smaller newsprint sizes, and Capitolium Headline 2 for compact multi-line headlines with narrowed lettershapes. Nineteen styles in total, with small caps, four numeral sets and full Latin A language support across all weights.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Capitolium 2 as a modern and indestructible serif with strong links to tradition, noting that Unger's original wayfinding design was too delicate for newsprint and required sturdier shapes, a taller x-height and more characters per line. The revised family adds over 350 glyphs per weight, refined kerning and screen optimisation, and was recognised by ATypI's Letter.2 competition and Typographica's Best Typefaces of 2011.

name: Catalpa
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Azza Alameddine
classification_notes: Explicitly designed as a headline-only display family with no regular weights, drawing on the visual language of wood type. Its extreme weight polarisation and geometric quirks place it firmly outside body text categories.
summary: A wood-type-inspired display family by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, built exclusively for headlines across eight extreme weights with no middle ground.
description: Deliberately stripped of any regular weights, Catalpa splits its eight styles into four very light cuts and four hulking heavy ones, with variable fonts at each end of the spectrum for fine-tuning. Geometric influences are most visible in the lighter weights, while the bold end widens and thickens in the manner of nineteenth-century wood type, with details like reversed-contrast S and angular M, W and Q adding character. It won a Gold Indigo Award in 2021 and forms the first part of a TypeTogether editorial trilogy designed to cover the full range of publication weight needs.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Catalpa as a wood-type-inspired headline family by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, made to overwhelm and bellow rather than to inform. It carries eight styles split across an extreme light and an extreme heavy range, each with its own variable font, and sits as the headlining bookend of a three-family editorial trilogy released through 2020.

name: Chaco
classification_notes: Chaco is a humanist sans with roots in wayfinding research, featuring inktraps, spacious internal angles, and carefully calibrated proportions that place it firmly in the humanist tradition rather than geometric or grotesque camps.
summary: Designed by Rubén Fontana from Argentine road-signage research, this seven-weight humanist sans carries inktraps, spacious apertures and carefully weighted verticals into branding, wayfinding and editorial text.
description: Originating as a wayfinding and roadway signage project for Argentina and seven additional countries, Chaco was engineered to remain legible at distance, in small sizes and in reversed-out printing. Refined proportions, functional inktraps and a specially designed 'ch' digraph for South American languages give it a distinctive character beyond the generic humanist sans. The family spans seven weights with matching italics, covers over 150 Latin-based languages plus Vietnamese, and includes stylistic sets for serifed Roman numeral forms alongside weight-matched arrows, symbols and geometric shapes.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Chaco as combining aesthetic needs for identity work, international wayfinding research, and the extreme detail required for small text. Rubén Fontana's design originated as a signage system drawing on standards from eight countries, then expanded to cover headlines and paragraph text with narrower proportions, spacious internal angles, inktraps, and carefully differentiated numerals. The family covers seven weights plus italics with support for over 150 Latin-based languages and Vietnamese, and includes symbols and arrows matched to each typographic weight.

name: Cora
classification_notes: Cora is a sans serif with stroke contrast and classically proportioned capitals drawn from Roman inscriptions, placing it in the humanist sans tradition rather than a neutral grotesque or geometric camp.
summary: A humanist sans by Bart Blubaugh with stroke contrast, a large x-height and classically proportioned capitals, designed for legibility at small sizes on screen and in print.
description: Bart Blubaugh drew Cora with an extra-large x-height and a degree of stroke contrast uncommon in utilitarian sans serifs, while the uppercase takes its proportions from ancient Roman inscriptions, making all-caps setting viable for titling. The instrokes taper at the start of each curve before widening onto the vertical stroke, softening what might otherwise be a severe geometric construction. Six styles cover Regular, Medium and Bold with matching italics, and the family is pitched at text-heavy applications such as websites, directories and editorial layouts.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Cora as a versatile and highly legible sans serif that excels through an extra-large x-height, some stroke contrast and capitals inspired by classical lettering. They note its forthright shapes allow condensation or expansion without severe distortion, and suggest applications including website text, telephone directories, dictionaries and music liner notes. The family won first prize at the 2008 Granshan Type Design Competition.

name: Coranto 2
classification_notes: Derived from Gerard Unger's 1997 typeface Paradox, Coranto 2 is explicitly designed for newspaper text and headline setting, with a large x-height and sturdy letterforms characteristic of editorial newsfaces.
summary: A newspaper serif derived from Gerard Unger's Paradox, refined for running text and headline setting across demanding print conditions.
description: Based on Gerard Unger's 1997 typeface Paradox, the family splits into two sub-families: Coranto 2 for body text and Coranto 2 Headline, which carries a taller x-height and condensed proportions suited to tight newspaper column layouts. The OpenType release adds over 250 glyphs, full Latin A language support, small caps, four numeral sets and arbitrary fractions. In use at publications including De Tijd and Eskilstuna-Kuriren, it sits comfortably beyond newspaper contexts wherever a refined, high-legibility serif is needed.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Coranto 2 as a refined serif newsface arising from a desire to transfer the elegance of Gerard Unger's Paradox to newsprint, with a larger x-height and sturdier letterforms to meet the demands of newspaper printing. The Headline variant is more condensed and carries an even taller x-height for increased legibility in space-constrained layouts. The updated OpenType family covers multiple languages and has been optimised for varied screen use.

name: Crete
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a slab serif throughout; the unusual approach of varying serif shape rather than stem weight distinguishes it from conventional slab families.
summary: A slab serif by Veronika Burian that varies serif shape rather than stem weight to distinguish its three styles, inspired by wall lettering in a small Greek chapel.
description: Rather than stepping up stem widths across weights, Crete shifts the character of its serifs and terminals, producing Thin and Thick styles that are metrically interchangeable and can be mixed without text reflow. Crete Round departs further with revised terminals for a more contemporary feel. The family spans six styles including italics, is available under the SIL Open Source Licence via Google Fonts, and won first prize at the 2008 Granshan Type Design Competition.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Crete as a surprising and unconventional slab serif inspired by wall lettering in a small Grecian chapel, adjusted considerably by Veronika Burian to work in text as well as display. Each style name refers to changes in the serifs rather than stem weight, while Crete Round offers a more contemporary and functional alternative. The six-style family is metrically interchangeable across the upright styles and optimised for screen and print use.

name: Ebony
classification_notes: Ebony sits between humanist sans and grotesque, combining open counters and generous terminals with the regularised rhythm of a grotesque. The designers explicitly describe it as seeking balance between these two traditions.
summary: A 18-style sans family by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione that balances humanist openness with grotesque regularity, weighted toward the bolder end of the spectrum.
description: First sketched in 2008 and released in 2016, Ebony runs from Thin to Ultrablack across 18 styles, with italics throughout and a variable font option for web deployment. The dark weights carry taut, high-contrast curves suited to headlines and branding, while lighter weights hold up in continuous text with wide stances and open counters. Character support extends to Vietnamese and capital German eszett, and quirky details like oldstyle numerals and an unconventional @ symbol give it personality beyond its editorial origins.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Ebony as a high-velocity sans serif with a bold voice and daring curves, designed to perform across branding, advertising and editorial contexts. Originally sketched in 2008 by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, it was developed into an 18-style family with variable font support and expanded character sets including Vietnamese, combining the legibility of humanist sans serifs with the structure of grotesques.

name: Edita
classification_notes: Edita is classified as a humanist serif, drawing on calligraphic forms particularly visible in its italics, with warm, fluid strokes suited to book and editorial typography.
summary: A warm, humanist serif by Pilar Cano designed for book typography where text sits alongside photographs and graphic elements.
description: Designed by Pilar Cano, Edita is a humanist serif with fluid, calligraphically informed strokes that are most evident in the italics. The family spans eight styles across three weights, including dedicated Small Text cuts with a taller x-height, heavier stems, reduced contrast and inktraps for use at sizes as small as five points. Versatile enough for novels, catalogues and promotional material, it has appeared in print work including the Metropolitan Museum of Art's 2019 Tale of Genji exhibition catalogue.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Edita as a contemporary book and magazine font family with a soft and warm voice, humanist in concept yet with a contemporary feel where softness and fluidity play a central role. The italics are loosely based on handwriting, and two optically corrected Small Text styles have been designed specifically for captions and notes at very small sizes, featuring a taller x-height, heavier stems, reduced contrast and inktraps.

name: Eskapade
classification_notes: The roman styles adopt a humanist oldstyle serif structure with low contrast and tall x-height; the companion Fraktur subfamily places the family in a hybrid category bridging humanist serif and blackletter traditions.
summary: A 16-font family by Alisa Nowak that pairs a condensed humanist oldstyle serif with a contemporary Fraktur companion, both sharing structural roots in the Cancellaresca script.
description: The roman set draws on humanist oldstyle conventions but sits more condensed than typical oldstyle serifs, a deliberate choice to achieve visual harmony when set alongside the Fraktur styles. Eskapade Fraktur offers proper italics derived from Cancellaresca and secondarily influenced by Sütterlin forms, a distinction rare among Fraktur typefaces which typically offer only slanted uprights. A second set of double-stroked decorative capitals in the Fraktur cuts extends its range into branding, logotypes and packaging. Named among Typographica's Best Typefaces of 2012.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Eskapade as the result of Alisa Nowak's research into Roman and German blackletter forms, with the goal of adapting Fraktur's broken letter structures into a contemporary family rather than reviving a historical model. The roman styles are designed for continuous text in books and magazines, while the Fraktur styles are positioned for headlines, branding and packaging, with the unusual addition of true italics based on the Cancellaresca script and influenced by Sütterlin forms.

name: Essay Text
classification_notes: Essay Text draws directly from French Renaissance roman letterforms of the 15th to early 17th centuries, with a slightly slanted roman, open and closed apertures, and calligraphic stroke origins — placing it firmly in the humanist/Garalde tradition.
summary: A two-style book typeface by Stefan Ellmer rooted in French Renaissance roman letterforms, with a slightly slanted roman and a near-upright italic that functions as a distinct text face.
description: Stefan Ellmer drew Essay Text with a slight slant in the roman, a combination of open and closed apertures, and organic strokes anchored to a fully straight baseline — qualities that give it a calm, readable rhythm for extended book setting. The italic departs from calligraphic convention with an unusually upright, formalised character that echoes the pre-mating era of Renaissance type, where roman and italic coexisted as separate faces. Awarded recognition from Communication Arts, Typographica, Fontshop and the European Design Awards in 2014, the family covers multiple languages and has been optimised for screen.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Essay Text as an elegant serif family with many stylistic alternates and typographic niceties, designed for book setting. The typeface takes its name from the literary sense of the word — an attempt or trial — positioning it as an attempt to express ideas about reading, the omnipresence of history, and the tension between calligraphic origins and a designer's personal idiom. The regular looks to the spirit of the French Renaissance, while the italic takes a more formalised, upright approach with minimal cursiveness, functioning as a distinct secondary text face.

name: Fino Sans
classification_notes: Fino Sans is the sans-serif counterpart to a Rational/Modern display serif system with strong Didone contrast between thick and thin strokes, designed explicitly for titling and editorial display use rather than text setting.
summary: A high-contrast display sans by Ermin Međedović that brings the theatrical drama of Didone serifs into a sans-serif format, available in two optical sizes across six weights with italics.
description: Part of a three-member type system alongside Fino and Fino Stencil, Fino Sans translates the sharp, narrow proportions and pronounced thick-to-thin contrast of Rational display type into a sans-serif register. Two optical sizes, Sans and Title, separate workhorse display settings from the finest hairlines reserved for the largest headlines. Alternate uppercase characters swap narrow ovals for full circular forms, giving designers a way to modulate between restrained elegance and full theatrical extravagance within the same setting.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Fino Sans as part of a dramatic, contemporary Didone titling system by Ermin Međedović that amplifies the genre's most theatrical aspects while offering uncommon flexibility of style and variation. The family is aimed particularly at editorial design, offers six weights with italics in two optical sizes, and covers Latin-based languages from standard English to Eastern European accents. It was listed among Fontshop's best typefaces of 2017.

name: Fino Stencil
classification_notes: A high-contrast Didone titling face in stencil form, designed explicitly for display and editorial use; the extreme thick-thin contrast and theatrical proportions place it firmly in the display category with a Didone subcategory distinction.
summary: A stencil cut of Ermin Međedović's Fino type system, bringing the theatrical high-contrast of Didone titling into a stencil format across six weights with matching italics and two optical sizes.
description: Part of the three-branch Fino system alongside Fino and Fino Sans, the Stencil cut takes the narrow proportions, sharp hairlines and bold stems of a Rational display serif and interrupts them with stencil breaks. Six weights from Ultrathin to Bold run in both a standard text size and a Title cut with finer hairlines for larger settings, giving editorial designers 24 styles to work across short display copy and full-scale headlines. Alternate round forms for C, D, G, O and Q, plus a ligature set, extend the range of typographic gesture available.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Fino Stencil as a dramatic, contemporary Didone titling family that taps into archetypes to scale beyond mere aesthetics. Designed by Ermin Međedović, the family amplifies the most theatrical aspects of the Didone genre while offering uncommon flexibility, particularly for editorial design. The stencil version pairs with the serif and sans cuts to form a complete type system, with each branch covering six weights, matching italics and two optical sizes for settings ranging from 24pt text to large-scale titles.

name: Futura®100 Arabic
designer: Azza Alameddine, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione (lead concept), Paul Renner (original)
classification_notes: An Arabic interpretation of Futura's geometric sans-serif principles, rendered in Naskh style with low contrast and minimalist construction faithful to Renner's original geometric rationale.
summary: A Naskh-style Arabic companion to the centenary revival of Futura, designed by Azza Alameddine and grounded in two years of archival research into Renner's original geometric intentions.
description: Designed by Azza Alameddine under the direction of Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, this Arabic cut of Futura®100 brings Renner's geometric principles into a minimalist Naskh form suited to contemporary digital and print publishing. Twelve styles span six weights with an optical size axis distinguishing text and display use, and a variable font is available covering the full weight and optical size range. It forms part of a 23-script global expansion released in concert with Bauer Types, making Futura available for genuine multinational deployment for the first time in its century-long history.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Futura®100 Arabic as a minimalist and versatile Naskh for today's digital tools and multiscript use, developed after more than two years of archival research to honour Renner's original intentions. Released as part of the broader Futura®100 project spanning 23 scripts with six weights and optical size variations in each, it is positioned as a historically faithful yet digitally optimised tool for agencies and brands with global reach.

name: Futura®100 Armenian
designer: Paul Renner, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Gor Jihanian
classification_notes: A faithful revision and expansion of Paul Renner's 1927 geometric sans-serif Futura, maintaining its defining circular forms and optical precision while extending coverage to Armenian script and 22 additional writing systems.
summary: A scholarly revision of Paul Renner's 1927 Futura extended to Armenian script, part of a 23-script global expansion developed with Bauer Types over more than two years of archival research.
description: Developed in partnership with Bauer Types, Futura®100 Armenian is the Armenian-script component of a planned 23-script family that aims to make Renner's geometric sans genuinely usable across the majority of the world's languages. The Armenian portion was drawn by Gor Jihanian and offers six weights from Light to Bold, each with matching obliques, plus a Text subfamily with an optical size axis to improve readability at small sizes. Case-sensitive punctuation, small caps, five figure sets and script-specific alternates complete a rigorous typographic offering that goes well beyond a simple adaptation.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Futura®100 Armenian as part of a century-defining expansion of Paul Renner's original design, built after more than two years of archival research and letterform comparison. Working under licence from Bauer Types, they position the 23-script family as one of the few truly global typefaces, with Armenian among the first ten scripts released alongside obliques and an optical size axis. They frame it not as an evolution but as a true expansion across time that honours Renner's original intention.

name: Futura®100 Cyrillic
designer: Paul Renner, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Vera Evstafieva
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif in the direct lineage of Renner's original 1927 Futura, built on circular forms and minimal stroke contrast. The Cyrillic cut extends the same geometric rationality into the Cyrillic script.
summary: A rigorously researched Cyrillic expansion of Renner's 1927 Futura, designed by Vera Evstafieva as part of TypeTogether's 23-script global release.
description: The Cyrillic cut is one of twelve scripts released in the first phase of Futura®100, a project that spent over two years in archival research before cutting a single glyph. Six weights run from Light to Bold, each with a matching oblique, and an optical size axis adjusts spacing and extender lengths for text versus display settings. The broader Futura®100 family is designed for multinational brands and publishers who need a single geometric typeface that holds across more than 90% of the world's written languages.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Futura®100 as not an evolution of Renner's original but a true expansion across time — a project developed in concert with Bauer Types and involving specialists across 23 scripts, two years of archival research, and a goal of making Futura genuinely usable for global branding, digital devices, and modern publishing demands.

name: Futura®100 Georgian
designer: Paul Renner, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Ana Sanikidze
classification_notes: A direct expansion of Renner's 1927 geometric sans-serif Futura, retaining its low-contrast geometric construction while adding optical size variants and obliques for the Georgian script.
summary: A carefully researched Georgian-script extension of Paul Renner's Futura, released as part of TypeTogether's ambitious 23-script global expansion of the centenary typeface.
description: Ana Sanikidze drew the Georgian cuts under the lead direction of Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, following more than two years of archival research into Renner's original 1927 design. The family ships in six weights from Light to Bold, each with matching obliques, plus a variable font with weight and optical size axes that adjust spacing and extender length for smaller text. Intended for multinational branding and publishing where Georgian text must sit alongside Latin, Cyrillic and the other scripts in the Futura®100 system.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Futura®100 Georgian as part of their centenary revision of Paul Renner's original geometric sans, developed in collaboration with Bauer Types and released across 23 scripts to cover more than 90% of the world's population. The Georgian edition, drawn by Ana Sanikidze, offers 24 styles including obliques and optical size variants, and is positioned for brands and agencies needing faithful Futura geometry across global publishing and digital platforms.

name: Futura®100 Hebrew
designer: Tom Grace, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif rooted in Paul Renner's original Futura, extended to the Hebrew script. Maintains the core minimalist geometric principles of the 1927 design while adapting proportions and forms for Hebrew letterforms.
summary: A Hebrew script extension of Paul Renner's Futura, developed as part of a 23-script global expansion led by TypeTogether in partnership with Bauer Types.
description: Drawn by Tom Grace under the lead direction of Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, the Hebrew family delivers six weights from Light to Bold, each with matching obliques, across two optical sizes for display and text setting. It forms part of Futura®100, TypeTogether's wider revision of Renner's 1927 geometric sans, grounded in over two years of archival research to align the design with the original intent. The Text subfamily adjusts spacing and extender length via an optical size axis, making smaller settings more legible across digital and print media.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Futura®100 Hebrew as a Hebrew script interpretation of the iconic and warm geometric font family, developed in partnership with Bauer Types as part of Futura®100 — a 23-script expansion of Renner's original 1927 design. After more than two years of archival research, the release aims to be more accurate, more powerful, and better aligned with digital publishing needs, with language support targeting more than 90% of the world's population by the end of 2026.

name: Futura®100 Lao
designer: Suppakit Chalermlarp, Smich Smanloh, Anuthin Wongsunkakon, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: A monolinear, geometric interpretation of Paul Renner's Futura in the Lao script, maintaining the rational, low-contrast forms of the original geometric sans tradition.
summary: A Lao-script companion to Futura®100, designed by Suppakit Chalermlarp under the direction of Smich Smanloh and Anuthin Wongsunkakon, carrying the geometric rationality of Renner's original into the Lao writing system.
description: Part of TypeTogether's 23-script expansion of Paul Renner's Futura, the Lao variant offers six weights from Light to Bold, each with a matching oblique, plus variable font versions covering both upright and oblique axes. An optical size axis adjusts spacing and extender length for smaller text, addressing the practical demands of digital publishing in Lao. Designed as a faithful monolinear interpretation rather than a loose adaptation, it sits within a family intended for multinational branding and publishing where script consistency across languages is required.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Futura®100 Lao as a rational and monolinear Lao interpretation for the iconic multiscript family, developed after more than two years of archival research to honour Paul Renner's original intention. The wider Futura®100 project spans 23 scripts with six weights each, aiming to bring language support to more than 90% of the world's population, with Lao among the first ten scripts released alongside obliques and an optical size axis.

name: Futura®100 PanAfrican
designer: Paul Renner, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Yorlmar Campos
classification_notes: A direct revision and expansion of Renner's original 1927 Futura, retaining its defining geometric construction and low contrast; the PanAfrican variant extends the Latin family with script-specific support for African languages.
summary: A carefully researched revision of Paul Renner's 1927 Futura, extended into PanAfrican Latin with six weights, obliques and an optical size axis for African-language publishing and global branding.
description: This PanAfrican variant is part of TypeTogether's broader Futura®100 project, which draws on more than two years of archival research to realign the design with Renner's original intentions while meeting current digital demands. The family ships in six weights from Light to Bold, each with a matching oblique, plus Text variants governed by an optical size axis that adjusts spacing and extender lengths for small-scale reading. Developed in collaboration with Bauer Types and consultant Taurai Valerie Mtake, it addresses a long-standing gap in language support for African scripts and is designed for agencies and publishers working across sub-Saharan markets.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Futura®100 PanAfrican as part of a landmark revision of Paul Renner's original geometric sans, created after two years of archival research and released in partnership with Bauer Types. They frame it as a true expansion rather than an evolution, covering 23 scripts by the end of 2026 and offering language support for more than 90% of the world's population, with the PanAfrican Latin cut addressing a significant historical gap in the family's script coverage.

name: Futura®100 Thai
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Smich Smanloh, Knaz Uiyamathiti, Anuthin Wongsunkakon (Thai); Paul Renner (original)
classification_notes: A low-contrast, monolinear geometric sans based directly on Paul Renner's 1927 Futura, extended to Thai script. The design adheres to the geometric construction principles of the original while adapting forms for Thai-script conventions.
summary: The Thai extension of TypeTogether's meticulous 23-script expansion of Paul Renner's 1927 Futura, released in six weights with matching obliques and an optical size axis.
description: Developed over more than two years of archival research, Futura®100 Thai is part of a project to bring Renner's geometric sans to over 90% of the world's population across 23 scripts. The Thai cut, art directed by Anuthin Wongsunkakon and Smich Smanloh, follows the original's low-contrast, monolinear construction while respecting Thai script conventions. Six weights from Light to Bold, each with an oblique, are supported by an optical size axis to improve readability at small sizes, alongside typographic features including case-sensitive punctuation and script-specific alternates.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Futura®100 as a revision and expansion of Renner's original, made in partnership with Bauer Types and released across 23 scripts by 2026. They frame it not as an evolution but as a true expansion across time, honouring Renner's intentions while updating the design for digital publishing, global usability and multinational branding. The Thai variant is one of ten scripts to include obliques, with an optical size axis for enhanced reading at text sizes.

name: Garalda
classification_notes: Garalda is explicitly described as a humanist slab serif, featuring rectangular serifs drawn from Garamond heritage but with a mechanised regularity that blends humanist calligraphic proportions with squared terminals.
summary: A Garamond revival by Xavier Dupré that introduces squared serifs and swash italics to produce a contemporary humanist slab for book and editorial setting.
description: Dupré's starting point was a low-resolution scan of the rare Tory-Garamond, thought to be the Ollière Garamond cut of 1914, which gave him the freedom to reinterpret rather than trace. The result sits between Garalde and slab: rectangular serifs keep a mechanised regularity at text sizes, while cursive italic ligatures and swashes drawn from multiple historic Garamonds animate display work. Eight styles run from Regular to Bold with matching italics, including ornaments and broad multilingual support for book and pamphlet setting.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Garalda as a charming 21st-century family that renews a legacy of finesse, designed by Xavier Dupré. They note it reads as a workaday text face at paragraph sizes but reveals graphic character at display sizes, and classify it as a humanist slab serif whose rectangular serifs and italic swashes are rooted in rare early-twentieth-century Garamond sources.

name: Givry
classification_notes: Givry is explicitly modelled on the bâtarde flamande, a 15th-century French-Flemish writing hand that is historically classified within the blackletter tradition but is notably more fluid and calligraphic than fraktur or textura.
summary: A single-weight blackletter by Tom Grace rooted in the bâtarde flamande, a lively 15th-century French-Flemish writing hand prized for its fluidity and calligraphic rhythm.
description: Tom Grace researched the bâtarde flamande tradition to produce a blackletter that sits apart from the austerity of fraktur and textura: high-contrast yet delicate, with a slight rightward lean, swash characters and round forms fused for economy. Despite its expressive nature, Givry was engineered for continuous text as well as display, with OpenType features and support for more than forty languages. Named in Typographica's Best Typefaces of 2008.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Givry as a lively and delicate blackletter modelled on the bâtarde flamande, a dynamic writing style used in 15th-century France and present-day Belgium. They note its calligraphic flair, flowing rhythm and round forms distinguish it from its blackletter cousins, and that it was carefully refined to function as a continuous text face as well as an energetic display typeface, with OpenType support for more than forty languages.

name: Iskra
classification_notes: Low-contrast profile with brush lettering influences, slightly top-heavy asymmetry and warm letterform details place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition rather than geometric or grotesque.
summary: A 14-style humanist sans by Tom Grace that draws on brush lettering asymmetry to sit comfortably between utilitarian and decorative roles.
description: Designed by Tom Grace and released through TypeTogether, Iskra takes its name from the Russian word for spark, and earns it through a slightly top-heavy asymmetry and daring curves that reference brush lettering without abandoning legibility. Seven weights from Ultra Thin to Ultra Bold each come with matching italics, covering over 75 languages across Latin and Cyrillic scripts. It received a TDC Certificate of Excellence in Typeface Design in 2013 for both its Latin and Cyrillic work.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Iskra as a unique sans serif designed by Tom Grace to challenge the limits between utilitarian and decorative type. Sporting a low-contrast profile and available in 14 styles with Cyrillic and Latin support for over 75 languages, it draws on brush lettering asymmetry and economical proportions, making it a memorable choice for presentations, articles, branding and advertising. The full family has been optimised for varied screen uses.

name: Iskra Cyrillic
classification_notes: Iskra is a low-contrast sans serif with brush-lettering influences, top-heavy asymmetry and warm individual letterforms; its humanist warmth and calligraphic undercurrent place it firmly in the humanist sans subcategory.
summary: A low-contrast humanist sans by Tom Grace with brush-lettering inflections, available in 14 styles across Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
description: Iskra draws on the less predictable rhythms of brush lettering to introduce a slight top-heavy asymmetry and daring curves into an otherwise economical sans serif structure. Its 14 styles run from Ultrathin to Ultrabold with matching italics, covering over 75 languages across Latin and Cyrillic codepages. The family won a Type Directors Club Certificate of Excellence in 2013 for both scripts, and sits well in editorial, branding and advertising contexts where warmth and legibility need to coexist.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Iskra as a sans serif designed by Tom Grace to challenge the limits between utilitarian and decorative type. Available in Latin-only and Latin-plus-Cyrillic versions, the family spans 14 styles and supports over 75 languages. They note its low-contrast profile, brush-lettering tribute, and balanced rhythm over long text spans, positioning it as a stimulating choice for presentations, articles, branding and advertising.

name: Jockey
classification_notes: Jockey is a display sans inspired by Argentine poster lettering from the 1930s, featuring blunted, constructed letterforms typical of hand-drawn poster type rather than refined text faces.
summary: A free display sans revival based on 1930s Argentine horse racing poster lettering, developed by TypeTogether for Google Webfonts.
description: TypeTogether reconstructed a full character set from just a handful of surviving uppercase letterforms by an unknown Argentine poster artist who worked for horse racing venues in the 1930s. The result is a blunted, simply constructed sans that carries the directness of hand-drawn poster text. Available in two weights, Regular and Bold, it was commissioned by Google for its Webfonts project and is free to use.
foundry_blurb: Google commissioned TypeTogether to develop a poster type family for its Webfonts project. Working from the lettering of an unidentified Argentine artist who designed race track posters in the 1930s, the team expanded a small set of uppercase forms into a complete typeface, now available free through Google Webfonts.

name: Karmina
classification_notes: Karmina is a text serif designed specifically for demanding newspaper and pocket-book printing conditions, combining calligraphic influences with large inktraps and a high x-height — characteristics consistent with a robust text or newsface serif tradition.
summary: A text serif by José Scaglione and Veronika Burian engineered for pocket books and newspapers, with large serifs, prominent inktraps and a compressed x-height optimised for poor printing conditions.
description: Designed to hold up under high-speed web press printing on low-quality paper, Karmina uses generous serifs, sharp letterforms and pronounced inktraps at acute connections to maintain legibility where lesser typefaces break down. A large x-height paired with relatively compressed proportions keeps text economical without sacrificing readability, and the italic weights lean into the calligraphic detailing to distinguish text clearly from the roman. The family comes in four styles and has been used as the body typeface for the Polish daily newspaper Polska.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Karmina as a text typeface by José Scaglione and Veronika Burian created primarily for pocket books and budget editions, built to withstand the worst printing conditions including low-quality papers, high-speed web presses and variable ink levels. They highlight its large serifs for small reproduction sizes, sharp shapes, calligraphic influences and large inktraps, as well as its combination of a large x-height with compressed letterforms for space economy.

name: Ronnia
classification_notes: TypeTogether describe it explicitly as a humanist sans serif; its design choices including large x-height, open apertures and distinct letterforms are characteristic of the humanist sans tradition engineered for editorial legibility.
summary: A humanist sans serif by José Scaglione and Veronika Burian, built for newspaper and magazine applications with a large x-height and a broad range of 28 styles.
description: Designed primarily for editorial use, Ronnia balances strong impact in heavier weights with clear legibility in lighter ones, covering regular and condensed widths across seven weights each with matching italics. Key letterforms such as the 'a', 'g' and 'G' carry enough open space for quick recognition and enough personality to sustain continuous reading. OpenType features including small caps, ornaments, info-numerals, fractions and stylistic alternates address the specific demands of complex information and editorial design.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Ronnia as a humanist sans designed by José Scaglione and Veronika Burian, engineered primarily for newspaper and magazine use. Its space-saving ability, high legibility and hospitable tone are cited as central design intentions, with 28 styles across regular and condensed widths giving designers the range needed for complex editorial and information design tasks. The family has been optimised for screen and includes an extensive set of OpenType features.

name: Karmina Sans
classification_notes: Karmina Sans is a humanist sans with calligraphic origins smoothed out during development; it retains the proportions and warmth of its serif companion Karmina, placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A twelve-style humanist sans by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, designed to complement the serif Karmina while standing independently for editorial and corporate use.
description: Developed using multiple master technology with light and extrabold as interpolation poles, the family spans six weights from Light to Heavy, each with a matching italic. Middle weights are calibrated for extended reading in print and on screen, while the Heavy is described by its designers as one of the darkest and most powerful text weights available without sacrificing legibility. The family supports Latin A Extended with small caps, five sets of figures, ligatures and a full range of fractions.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Karmina Sans as a versatile and vivid text face developed by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione. It shares the basic proportions and technical excellence of its serifed sibling, Karmina, but offers greater flexibility for editorial designers. Extreme weights serve headlines and corporate identity programmes, while intermediate weights suit everything from short texts to long-form reading in digital or print contexts.

name: Lektorat
classification_notes: Lektorat draws on late 19th and early 20th century German grotesque families from Schelter & Giesecke, with distinctive raised shoulders, deep junctions, and asymmetrical counters placing it firmly in the grotesque tradition.
summary: A 48-style sans family by Florian Fecher built for editorial publishing, with six concentrated text weights and three display widths spanning Narrow, Condensed and Compressed.
description: Inspired by Schelter & Giesecke grotesques of the late 19th century, Lektorat carries raised shoulders, deep junctions and asymmetrical counters as its defining formal traits. The text subfamily keeps things deliberately spare — Regular, Semibold and Bold with obliques — while 42 display styles across three compressed widths handle everything from banners to decks and callouts. Four variable font files cover both weight and width axes for print and screen workflows. Winner of the 2019 Gerard Unger Scholarship and awarded Gold at the Indigo Awards 2021.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Lektorat as methodical in rationale and irrepressible in function, designed by Florian Fecher with supervisors Veronika Burian and José Scaglione. The family was built after thorough analysis of magazine publishing, drawing partial inspiration from German foundry Schelter & Giesecke's grotesque families of the late 19th and early 20th century. Its text offering is deliberately concise — six styles only — while 42 display styles across Narrow, Condensed and Compressed widths cover editorial expression at scale.

name: LFT Etica Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced sans-serif designed explicitly for coding and technical applications, with fixed-width spacing and dual italic styles — cursive and slanted alternates — placing it squarely in the monospaced category.
summary: The monospaced companion to Leftloft's Etica family, built for coding environments and graphic layouts where fixed-width type is needed without sacrificing warmth.
description: Designed by Milan-based Leftloft studio, it carries the open forms and instroke details of the broader Etica family into a fixed-width format suited to coding, captions and confined spaces. Ten styles across five weights are paired with two distinct italic approaches: a default cursive cut with a looped 'k', outstroke tails and a single-storey 'a', and a simpler slanted alternate for those who want less personality. Arrows and geometric shapes are included, extending its usefulness in technical and UI contexts.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe LFT Etica Mono as a third leg to the Etica family, designed by Leftloft as a coder's everyday font and a designer's tool for invoking a technical genre. They note it preserves the Etica family's DNA — the instroke on the 'c', the open 'a' — while adding the structure a coding typeface demands: fixed spacing, clear forms and density. The default italics introduce warmer cursive shapes, with slanted alternates available for a more neutral feel.

name: LFT Etica Sheriff
classification_notes: A slab serif extension of the LFT Etica sans family, with bracketed serifs and a high x-height. Designed to span editorial and display use across three widths.
summary: A 36-style slab serif companion to LFT Etica, spanning three widths from regular to compressed, all with matching italics.
description: Designed by Leftloft in collaboration with Octavio Pardo, LFT Etica Sheriff extends the LFT Etica sans family into slab serif territory across regular, condensed and compressed widths. Character details shift subtly with weight: the lowercase 'k' transitions from disconnected to attached strokes as weight increases, and the serifs on the 'x' are deliberately asymmetrical. A high x-height, shared with its sans companion, gives lighter weights a narrower appearance and heavier weights a wider stance, broadening its range across headline and body copy.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe LFT Etica Sheriff as a slab serif that began as commissioned work for a client already using LFT Etica, designed to span types of communication from firm to forgiving. The 36-style family covers light to extrabold across regular, condensed and compressed widths with matching italics, and has been optimised for varied screen uses.

name: LFT Iro Sans
classification_notes: A technical sans-serif designed for wayfinding and high-legibility contexts, with proportions and weight range suited to both signage and editorial use, aligning it with the humanist sans tradition despite its technical origins.
summary: A 38-style sans family by Milan's Leftloft Studio, originating from the San Siro stadium wayfinding project and extending into unicase, pictogram and display subfamilies.
description: Born from the wayfinding system of Milan's San Siro stadium, this family was built to remain legible in low-visibility conditions across awkward signage environments. It expands well beyond that brief: an attention-demanding unicase combines lowercase and capital forms, a matched pictogram set replaces the usual mismatched icon libraries, and Display Ultralight and Heavy styles handle headline work. Eight weights across text styles each carry Italic and Slanted variants, giving editors and designers two distinct oblique options.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe LFT Iro Sans as an expansive family solving wide-ranging challenges across branding, wayfinding, pictographic language and editorial use. Developed by Milan-based Leftloft Studio from the San Siro stadium project, it features a technically driven text face, a distinctive unicase, matched pictograms simplified for public-space use, and display styles designed as headline stunners — 38 fonts in total, optimised for contemporary screen environments.

name: Lisbeth
classification_notes: Lisbeth is an all-italic serif family with no roman counterpart, featuring soft angular curves, a twisted stroke, and a high-contrast display style. Its handwritten quality and visible stroke contrast place it firmly in the serif category, with the italic designation as its defining structural characteristic.
summary: An all-italic serif family by Louisa Fröhlich with five text weights and a high-contrast display style, designed with no roman counterpart from the outset.
description: Rather than treating the italic as a companion to a roman, Lisbeth exists entirely as an italic, with its five text weights offering soft angular curves and a subtle twisted stroke that hints at handwriting without surrendering legibility. A slightly condensed footprint and a generous x-height make it practical for longer passages, while the separate display style pushes the twisted stroke to an extreme for decorative headlines. The character set includes proportional lining and oldstyle figures, tabular figures, fractions, ligatures and contextual alternates, making it a fully equipped tool for branding and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Lisbeth as a charming all-italic trailblazer designed by Louisa Fröhlich, with no roman style and a creation guided by imagination rather than restrictive writing tools. They highlight its internal vividness across branding and text use, noting the soft angularity of the text weights, a quirky high-contrast display style, and support for numerous languages with an extended Latin-A character set.

name: Literata
classification_notes: Literata is a screen-optimised serif with old-style influences, designed for digital text at multiple optical sizes. Its upright italic and variable font architecture place it firmly in the contemporary digital text serif tradition.
summary: A free, award-winning variable serif originally commissioned by Google for Play Books, now a 48-style digital publishing toolbox spanning Display to Caption optical sizes.
description: Built from scratch for version three, Literata is distributed as two variable font files covering roman and italic, with weight and optical size axes that span Display, Subhead, Text and Caption grades. Its upright italic is an uncommon choice for screen fonts, designed specifically to sidestep limitations of the pixel grid. The family covers Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, Vietnamese and PinYin, and is published under an Open Font Licence at no cost.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Literata 3 as a full redesign within variable font technology, conceived for intensive editorial use on screens of all kinds. Originally the custom typeface for Google Play Books, it is offered free under an Open Font Licence and contains more than 15 times the styles of a conventional OTF release, with support for Greek, Cyrillic, PinYin and Vietnamese.

name: Maiola
classification_notes: Maiola is a humanist serif drawing on oldstyle features and calligraphic influences, including a humanist axis, modest contrast and pen-formed terminals, placing it firmly in the humanist serif tradition with a contemporary expressive character.
summary: A humanist text serif by Veronika Burian, rooted in early Czech type design, with deliberate calligraphic irregularities that give the letterforms a hand-etched quality.
description: Designed by Veronika Burian and awarded at TDC 2004, Maiola draws on oldstyle conventions and calligraphic detail including a humanist axis, asymmetrical foot serifs, diagonally cut terminals and subtle stroke variation that reads as imperfection rather than error. The italic extends these qualities with greater contrast and sharper angularity, with alternate capitals available to shift the tone. Coverage spans Latin, Cyrillic and polytonic Greek, with the Greek extension drawn by Irene Vlachou, making the family well suited to multilingual publishing and literary work.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Maiola as a contemporary, expressive serif with calligraphic nuances, inspired by early Czech type design. They note its humanist axis, modest contrast and pen-formed terminals, and highlight how purposeful irregularities across the glyphs give the impression of hand-etching rather than digital construction. The family spans six styles and covers Latin, Cyrillic and polytonic Greek, with OpenType features throughout and optimisation for both print and screen.

name: Maiola Cyrillic
classification_notes: Maiola is a humanist serif with calligraphic origins, drawing on early Czech type design with a humanist axis, modest stroke contrast and pen-formed terminals that place it firmly in the humanist serif tradition.
summary: Veronika Burian's text serif with calligraphic roots, extended here to cover Cyrillic alongside Latin and polytonic Greek.
description: Inspired by early Czech type design, Maiola Cyrillic carries a handmade quality through purposeful irregularities: asymmetric foot serifs, diagonally cut terminals, and angled curves that give letterforms the feel of being etched rather than digitally constructed. Six styles span Regular, Book and Bold each with matching italics, where the italic cuts lean harder into sharp pen-tool angularity and greater stroke contrast. The family won first place in the Cyrillic category at the 2008 Granshan competition and has been recognised across multiple international awards for its balance of legibility and expressive personality.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Maiola Cyrillic as a contemporary typeface mindful of its heritage, designed for high-quality printed text. They highlight its oldstyle features and calligraphic reminiscences including a humanist axis, modest contrast and pen-formed terminals, noting that subtle irregularities in the glyphs enhance its dynamic spirit while remaining disciplined enough for continuous text. The Cyrillic extension harmonises with the Latin and polytonic Greek versions for multilingual settings.

name: Maiola Greek
classification_notes: Maiola Greek is a humanist serif with a calligraphic axis, modest stroke contrast, and pen-formed terminals inspired by early Czech type design. Its oldstyle features and handmade irregularities place it firmly in the humanist serif tradition.
summary: A humanist serif by Veronika Burian with Greek extension by Irene Vlachou, balancing calligraphic expressiveness with careful attention to legibility at text sizes.
description: Inspired by early Czech type design, Maiola Greek carries a handmade feeling through purposeful irregularities: asymmetrical foot serifs, diagonally cut terminals, and varying stroke weights that suggest an etched rather than digital origin. Six styles span Regular, Book and Bold with matching italics, the latter introducing sharper pen angles and alternate capitals. The family covers polytonic Greek and harmonises with the Latin and Cyrillic versions for multilingual settings.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Maiola Greek as a contemporary, expressive serif with calligraphic nuances, designed by Veronika Burian and extended to polytonic Greek by Irene Vlachou. The typeface draws on oldstyle features and a humanist axis to bridge tradition and progression, and is intended for high-quality printed text where legibility and personality are equally weighted. It has received recognition at TDC 2004, the 2005 Creative Review competition and the 2008 Granshan competition.

name: Marco
classification_notes: Marco draws directly from 15th-century Italian humanist letterforms in the tradition of Jenson and Manutius, with strong calligraphic disposition and angled asymmetric serifs characteristic of the humanist serif subcategory.
summary: A calligraphically-inflected humanist serif by Toshi Omagari, rooted in 15th-century Italian letterforms and built for intensive multilingual text setting.
description: Designed by Toshi Omagari, Marco takes its cues from Nicolas Jenson and Aldus Manutius, translating Renaissance humanist forms into a contemporary text family with angled asymmetric baseline serifs and slightly flared stems that push the eye forward through long passages. The italic styles are particularly developed, offering swashes, stylistic alternates, and over 2,600 characters per style, making them viable as display faces in their own right. Coverage extends across extended Latin, Cyrillic and polytonic Greek, with oldstyle and lining numerals, ligatures and fractions rounding out a substantial typographic feature set.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Marco as a lively humanist serif family for setting extensive multilingual texts, inspired by 15th-century Italian letterforms and designed by Toshi Omagari. They highlight its calligraphic disposition and angled asymmetric serifs as features that aid effortless reading, and point to the italic styles' swashes and stylistic alternates as connecting the family to a broader tradition of calligraphy. The family received an honorary diploma for excellence in Modern Cyrillic in 2014 and appeared in Tokyo TDC Vol 27.

name: Marco Cyrillic
classification_notes: Drawn from 15th-century Italian humanist models by Jenson and Manutius, with strong calligraphic disposition, angled asymmetric serifs and flared stems characteristic of humanist serif classification.
summary: A Cyrillic-extended humanist serif by Toshi Omagari, rooted in Italian Renaissance letterforms and built for demanding book and long-form reading.
description: Derived from the humanist tradition of Jenson and Manutius, the family offers six styles across three weights with matching italics that carry an unusually broad range of swashes and stylistic alternates. Over 1900 characters per roman style and nearly 2600 in the italics support extended Latin, Cyrillic and polytonic Greek, making it well suited to multilingual editions, literary publishing and complex editorial work. The italic cuts were deliberately drawn to contrast sharply with the roman, functioning independently as a display style when needed.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Marco Cyrillic as a lively humanist serif for setting extensive Cyrillic and multilingual texts, inspired by 15th-century Italian letterforms from Jenson and Manutius. They highlight its calligraphic disposition as ideal for intensive reading conditions such as epic poetry and complex plays, and point to its exceptional OpenType feature set including swashes, stylistic alternates, lining and oldstyle numerals, fractions and ligatures, with the italics designed to function both as emphasis and as standalone display styles. The family was awarded honorary diplomas for excellence in modern Cyrillic type design at Tokyo TDC Vol 27 in 2014.

name: Marco Greek
classification_notes: Rooted in 15th-century Italian humanist models by Jenson and Manutius, with strong calligraphic disposition, angled asymmetric serifs and flared stems characteristic of the humanist serif tradition.
summary: A polytonic Greek companion to Toshi Omagari's Marco, drawing on 15th-century Italian humanist models for sustained reading in Greek.
description: Rooted in the letterforms of Nicolas Jenson and Aldus Manutius, Marco Greek brings the lively calligraphic character of the broader Marco family to polytonic Greek typography. Six styles span Regular, Semibold and Bold with matching italics, the latter offering swashes and stylistic alternates that can function as display settings in their own right. The family was awarded honorary diplomas for excellence in modern Greek type design at the 2014 Tokyo TDC.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Marco Greek as a lively text face inspired by 15th-century Italian humanist letterforms, designed by Toshi Omagari. They highlight its strong calligraphic disposition, angled asymmetric serifs and flared stems as features suited to continuous and intensive reading, and note its extensive OpenType feature set including swashes, stylistic alternates and polytonic Greek support across six styles.

name: Molto
classification_notes: Molto is a slab serif with straight-edged construction and defined terminals, sitting squarely in the slab category. Its range from hairline to fat weights, combined with swash alternates, gives it a display-capable personality built on a traditional slab structure.
summary: A nine-weight slab serif by Xavier Dupré, spanning from a hairline display weight to a wide, punchy Fat, with swash capital alternates throughout.
description: Designed by Xavier Dupré, this slab serif runs from the runway-thin Hair weight to a maximum-density Fat, with the two extremes intended for display work in magazines, posters and titles. The seven intermediate weights hold their own at caption sizes through to headings, aided by notched diagonals, distinct numerals and acute terminals. Swash capital alternates add an unexpected flourish to what is otherwise a straight-edged, concrete-terminal construction.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Molto as a tonal master that tempers the traditional toughness of slab serifs with flourishes. Xavier Dupré's design updates the slab concept to allow a gentler range in lighter weights, while the extremes pack display punch. The family is positioned for multipurpose use across UI, video, print, packaging, advertising and branding, with swash alternates providing originality within the category.

name: Noam Text
classification_notes: A low-contrast text serif designed for bilingual Hebrew and Latin setting, with shallow horizontal curves and strong baseline serifs derived from Hebrew scribal tradition, placing it firmly in the text serif category.
summary: A dual-script Hebrew and Latin text serif by Adi Stern, designed as a contemporary alternative to the Frank-Rühl typefaces that have dominated Hebrew publishing since 1908.
description: Designed with the rigours of newspaper and book setting in mind, Noam Text balances a large x-height, open counters and low contrast to achieve legibility at small sizes and under poor printing conditions. The Latin and Hebrew scripts each remain authentic to their own traditions while sharing a coherent visual texture, with all punctuation supplied in Hebrew versions to keep each script self-contained. Three weights cover regular text, italic and bold, and the family has been programmed to handle mixed Hebrew, Latin, figures and punctuation without compromise.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Noam Text as a Hebrew and Latin serif built on its heritage with the twin tools of honour and progress, positioning it as a modern alternative to the Frank-Rühl fonts that have dominated the Hebrew book and newspaper market since 1908. They highlight its bilingual engineering, including opposing directionality, contrasting stress axes and fully dual-language punctuation, and note its discretion in avoiding both religious and secular stylistic extremes, making it suitable across books, newspapers and magazine design.

name: Noort
classification_notes: A text serif with low contrast, large x-height, long serifs and wide characters designed for information-dense editorial and multi-hierarchical settings; the analogue inktraps and ductus variation place it firmly in the humanist serif tradition.
summary: A TDC Award-winning editorial serif by Juan Bruce, built for dense information hierarchies with analogue inktraps, long serifs and a wide Latin Extended and Bengali character set.
description: Designed by Juan Bruce and awarded a TDC Award of Excellence in 2018, Noort pairs a large x-height and low contrast with long serifs and wide characters to hold up at small point sizes across complex, layered layouts. The roman and italic styles share transplanted details — the roman's serifs inform the italic's pace, while the italic's inktraps reappear in the upright structure — creating a coherent family that reads as both functional and considered. Ten fonts cover Latin A Extended, with a Bengali companion extending the family's reach to over 270 million additional readers.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Noort as a functional and fascinating serif designed for information designers who need to bring clarity and style to overwhelming amounts of content. They highlight its editorial text credentials — large x-height, ample spacing and low contrast — alongside its analogue details such as inktraps and a varying ductus that become a signature when set at subhead sizes. The complete family supports Latin Extended and pairs with a Bengali counterpart, with OpenType features including ligatures, fractions, multiple figure styles and swashes.

name: Noort Bengali
classification_notes: Noort Bengali is classified as a modern serif, characterised by strong serifs, tight curves and stylised angles echoed across both Bengali and Latin scripts, with calligraphic influences visible in terminals and curve-to-diagonal transitions.
summary: A multi-script serif pairing Bengali and Latin across five weights, designed by Juan Bruce for editorial, branding and display work at the scale of 270 million Bengali readers.
description: Noort Bengali extends Juan Bruce's award-winning Latin serif into the Bengali script, drawing formal correspondences between the strong serifs and stylised angles of the Latin and the terminals, knot forms and curve-to-diagonal transitions of the Bengali. The five-weight range from Regular to Extrabold covers both text and display needs, with OpenType contextual forms and variants built specifically for the script's positional requirements. Purchasing the Bengali includes the Latin styles, making it a ready-paired multiscript family for books, magazines, packaging and brand identity.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Noort Bengali as the first Bengali typeface in their retail library, released four years after the original Noort Latin. They frame it as a modern offering covering text and display needs for around 270 million people, with harmony between the two scripts achieved by giving each its own DNA and aesthetic value while maintaining shared formal principles. The typeface is positioned for travel guides, editorial publications, branding and dynamic advertising, on both screen and in print.

name: Playpen Sans
designer: Veronika Burian, Laura Meseguer, José Scaglione
classification_notes: Playpen Sans imitates casual handwriting using multiple alternates per character and a built-in shuffler; it falls firmly into the script/casual category despite offering extended text weights.
summary: A casual handwriting typeface with seven alternates per character and a built-in shuffler to prevent repetition, distributed free across eight weights.
description: Designed by Veronika Burian, Laura Meseguer and José Scaglione, Playpen Sans addresses the core problem of casual fonts by providing seven drawn variants for each character alongside an automatic shuffler that prevents any single shape from repeating in close proximity. The family spans eight weights from Thin to ExtraBold, includes emoji stickers and covers over 150 Latin-based languages. It targets greeting cards, children's books, educational materials and graphic novels, and ships as a variable font for precise weight control.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Playpen Sans as excelling at imitating casual handwriting with a completely natural look, combining the aesthetic of something made by hand with the digital function of a professional typeface. They built seven character alternates and a built-in shuffler to solve the core problem of casual fonts lacking enough variation to appear authentic, and the family supports over 150 Latin-based languages, includes emoji stickers and is available free across eight weights including a variable version.

name: Ploquine
classification_notes: A slab serif explicitly rooted in wood type history, with squared forms, flat internal counters in heavier weights, and curved serif connections characteristic of the slab genre.
summary: A slab serif by Emma Marichal drawing on wood type history, built across 14 styles for editorial and branding work at any scale.
description: Emma Marichal's Ploquine runs from Thin to ExtraBold with matching italics, each weight growing into its squarish forms and flattened internal counters as the optical mass increases. Unusual horizontal terminals on letters like 'a' and 't' give running text a forward rhythm, while extra-large capitals and punctuation act as deliberate style partners at display sizes. Winner of the 2022 Gerard Unger Scholarship, the family is suited to magazine text, museum catalogues, logotypes and poster-scale headlines alike.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Ploquine as a calm and structured slab serif for editorial and branding, based on historical wood types and designed by Emma Marichal. They position it as a go-to family that can speak in multiple voices, assertive in heavier weights with flattened counters that evoke wood type, and refined in lighter weights with delicate italics and curved serif connections. Display styles are listed as forthcoming.

name: Pollen
classification_notes: Pollen is a text serif with clearly calligraphic origins, shaped by broad-nib pen and brush work. Its low stroke contrast, curved serifs and informal letterforms like the double-storey 'g' place it firmly in the calligraphic serif tradition rather than transitional or humanist.
summary: A calligraphically rooted text serif by Eduardo Berliner, winner of the 2011 ISTD Premier Award, built for extended reading across print and screen.
description: Designed by Eduardo Berliner through a process combining calligraphy, pencil drawing and digital refinement, Pollen carries its broad-nib pen origins in its curved bottom serifs, fluid terminals and informally drawn diagonals. The family is a focused four-style set — Regular, Italic, Bold and Bold Italic — supported by a full suite of OpenType features including small caps, ligatures, ornaments, fractions and stylistic alternates. Its wide stance and low stroke contrast give it warmth and readability suited to fiction, magazines and advertising alike.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Pollen as a poetically joyful text typeface from calligraphic roots, designed by Eduardo Berliner to balance technical excellence with charm and personality. They note calligraphy's central role in shaping its construction, particularly the top serifs, curved bottom serifs and fluid terminals, with the glyphs further refined through pencil drawing and digital reinterpretation. The family's informal feel is intended to serve a wide range of applications from websites and magazines to fiction books.

name: Poltik
classification_notes: Poltik is a reverse contrast sans serif, where stroke weight is redistributed to horizontal strokes rather than verticals, a convention associated with display and experimental type. The Display cut pushes further into expressive territory with brutalist influences drawn from 1970s clock numerals.
summary: A reverse contrast sans serif with a matching display cut, both rooted in a 1970s clock face found in the designer's grandfather's drawer.
description: Patrycja Walczak's Poltik won the 2023 Gerard Unger Scholarship and comprises ten text styles across five weights with matching italics, plus a separate display cut in regular and italic. The text styles carry all their weight in the horizontal strokes, giving the family a distinctive reversed contrast rhythm that grows more characterful at heavier settings. The display cut is the brasher sibling, built from the ten numerals of a vintage clock and pushed toward brutalist, experimental territory suited to posters, packaging and branding. Two variable fonts cover the full weight and italic ranges.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Poltik as a funky, boisterous reversed contrast sans serif family that refuses to tuck away discreetly. Inspired by a 1970s clock design found in Walczak's grandfather's drawer, the display cut is an expressive, experimental headliner for large formats, while the text styles offer a readable, reliable reversed contrast sans that reveals more character the bolder it gets. The family is positioned for concept stores, magazines, album covers, posters and branding, in both digital and physical contexts.

name: Portada
classification_notes: Portada is a humanist serif engineered specifically for screen legibility, with wide proportions, ample x-height and sturdy serifs that allow it to function comparably to a sans in restrictive digital environments.
summary: A screen-first serif by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, built to match the legibility of a sans in restrictive digital environments.
description: Designed from the ground up for e-ink, harsh pixel grids and Retina displays, the family pairs eight text styles with ten titling styles and a free icon set. Wide proportions, generous x-height and sturdy serifs keep it steady where other serifs falter, while the italics carry a trace of calligraphic energy without tipping into ornamentation. A variable font version is now available, and the family has been applied across digital magazines, newspapers, UI and data-intensive apps.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether position Portada as a rare serif built for long-form screen reading and superlative UI performance, describing it as the serif match for restrictive digital environments where sans serifs have traditionally dominated. Created by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, the family uses sturdy serifs, wide proportions, ample x-height and clear interior negative space to put that old trope to rest, while the italics add a touch of calligraphic brio without compromising digital clarity. A complete icon set is included as a free download.

name: Portada Arabic
classification_notes: A Naskh-style Arabic serif with higher contrast than typical screen typefaces, engineered for digital legibility and extended reading — the Arabic expansion of the Latin Portada serif family.
summary: A Naskh-style Arabic serif by Sahar Afshar, built expressly for screen legibility across UI, apps and continuous reading without the low-contrast compromises typical of digital Arabic type.
