description: Portada Arabic challenges the assumption that screens demand low-contrast Arabic fonts, offering a higher-contrast Naskh with balanced letterforms, clear interior space and generous character height. Four text styles carry wider spacing for warm paragraphs in print, while six display styles reduce individual character width to preserve digital clarity. The family comes with two variable fonts, free icon sets in dark and light versions, and supports Arabic, Persian, Urdu, Dari, Aimaq, Dehwari, Talysh and Qashqa'i.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Portada Arabic as an expansion of the Portada family, designed by Sahar Afshar for superlative performance in long-form screen reading, UI and apps, while remaining warm in print. Engineered from the Naskh style, it addresses common screen-font problems — unstable structures, inconsistent pixel grids and space constraints — and supports Persian, Urdu, Dari and several other scripts alongside Arabic. It is also available in variable font format, with a free accompanying icon set.

name: Portada Thai
designer: Pilar Cano, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: A looped Thai serif with a matching Latin script, engineered specifically for screen reading in UI and long-form digital contexts. The looped construction and wide proportions distinguish it from loopless Thai designs and align it with the serif tradition.
summary: A looped Thai and Latin serif designed by Pilar Cano for sustained reading on screens, covering UI, apps and digital publications with wide proportions and clear interior spacing.
description: One of very few looped Thai typefaces built from the ground up for digital environments, Portada Thai works across e-ink, standard grids and Retina displays without losing legibility. The family separates into text and titling styles: text cuts prioritise openness and stability at small sizes, while titling styles introduce slightly reduced widths and restrained expressiveness. The complete bundle includes the full Latin script and a two-style icon set, making it a coherent multiscript system for developers and editorial teams alike.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Portada Thai as a rarity for screens — a looped Thai family by Pilar Cano (with the original Latin by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione) built for long-form reading and superlative UI performance. They note that modern looped styles must save space, carry a contemporary tone, and remain stable across changing pixel grids, and position Portada Thai as the looped option for restrictive digital environments. The complete family includes four text styles, six titling styles, a two-style icon set, and a full matching Latin script.

name: Postea
classification_notes: Explicitly rooted in Bauhaus geometric traditions, Postea is a geometric sans-serif with constructed letterforms balanced by humanist details, placing it firmly in the geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A geometric sans by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione that tempers Bauhaus restraint with lyrical detailing across seven weights, variable fonts and five scripts.
description: Designed at TypeTogether by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, Postea takes the constructed rigour of German geometric sans-serifs and softens it with deliberate variation in character widths, a serpentine 's', rotund 'o' and 'g', and weight-specific spacing tuned for both headline and text settings. Fourteen styles plus two variable fonts are accompanied by an icon system activated via stylistic set SS10 using a colon-bookend syntax, covering wayfinding, social interface and sanitary symbols. Script coverage extends to Arabic, Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew and Latin including Vietnamese, making it a credible choice for multinational branding and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Postea as a lyrical take on geometric sans-serifs, beginning with Bauhaus virtues of restraint and sparse appearance but injecting life through deliberate character-width play and purposeful details. They position it as equally suited to paragraphs and headings, with spacing calibrated per weight, a full icon family with an innovative activation method, and coverage across Arabic, Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew and Latin scripts.

name: Postea Cyrillic
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Vera Evstafieva
classification_notes: Postea is explicitly described as a take on German geometric typefaces, with midcentury values of simplicity and restraint; the Cyrillic cut extends that geometric framework with Cyrillic-specific construction.
summary: A geometric sans rooted in midcentury German models, adapted for Cyrillic by Vera Evstafieva and part of a five-script family covering Arabic, Greek, Hebrew and Latin.
description: Conceived by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione as a reappraisal of German geometric type, Postea Cyrillic brings deliberate variation in character widths that prevents the family from feeling austere. Hairline spacing is tighter for display use, while regular weights open up for comfortable reading at text sizes. The 14 styles, two variable fonts and an icon system with a novel colon-activation method make it unusually complete for multiscript branding, signage and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Postea Cyrillic as part of a multiscript family born of classic midcentury values of simplicity and impact, shaped by Burian and Scaglione's take on German geometric typefaces and reshaped for Cyrillic by Vera Evstafieva. They note its deliberate play between character widths, script-specific spacing logic, stylistic sets with geometric alternates, and an all-new icon family, positioning it for branding, signage, corporate and magazine use across five global scripts.

name: Postea Greek
designer: Veronika Burian, José Scaglione, Tom Grace, Yorlmar Campos
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a geometric sans, drawing on Bauhaus-era restraint while incorporating calligraphic Greek structure. The lively variation in character widths and attention to paragraph setting push it beyond purely constructed geometry.
summary: A geometric sans designed specifically for Greek, part of TypeTogether's five-script Postea family, balancing Bauhaus restraint with the lyrical structure of Greek calligraphy.
description: Designed by Veronika Burian, Tom Grace and Yorlmar Campos with consultation from Irene Vlachou, Postea Greek adapts the geometric logic of the broader Postea family to the specific demands of Greek script. Fourteen styles span seven weights from Hairline to Extrabold with matching italics, plus two variable font files covering the full weight axis. Hairline styles carry tighter spacing for display use, while text weights open up slightly for comfortable reading, and stylistic sets add geometric alternates, ornaments and an icon system activated via colon-bracketed keywords.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Postea Greek as a geometric sans reshaped with the right attributes for paragraphs, headings, branding and signage, built on Greek calligraphic structure refined through a century of lessons from geometric type design. They highlight its deliberate play between character widths as a source of liveliness and distinction, and position it as the rational, lyrical response to geometric sans serifs within a wider five-script family covering Arabic, Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew and Latin.

name: Postea Hebrew
designer: Tom Grace, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans serif with midcentury influences, designed to match the rhythm and colour of the Latin Postea family while incorporating Hebrew's constructed and human letterform qualities.
summary: A geometric Hebrew sans designed by Tom Grace as a companion to TypeTogether's Postea Latin, balancing constructed letterform geometry with the organic qualities of Hebrew script.
description: Developed as a Hebrew extension of Veronika Burian and José Scaglione's Postea Latin, the family spans seven upright weights from Hairline to Extrabold, with a variable font axis covering the full range. Hairline spacing is tighter for headline use, whilst the regular weights carry looser spacing suited to continuous text. Stylistic sets offer alternate character forms, including harder bowl variants and a single-storey 'a', and a companion icon family with wayfinding and interface symbols is activated through a colon-based naming syntax. The full Postea system covers Arabic, Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew and Latin, making it well suited to multilingual branding, signage and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Postea Hebrew as the rational response for pixel or paper Hebrew text, built on midcentury geometric virtues and designed to match the Latin Postea's colour and rhythm for unified multiscript typography. Tom Grace led the Hebrew design with consultancy from Meir Sadan, and the family aims to combine geometric and handmade structures for a readable, familiar and novel result across branding, signage, corporate typefaces and magazines.

name: Protipo
classification_notes: A low-contrast grotesque sans with round-rect construction and minimal stroke variation, designed explicitly for information design environments. The four-width system and icon sets place it firmly in the functional grotesque tradition.
summary: A low-contrast grotesque by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione built across four widths, seven weights and a two-weight icon set for demanding information design contexts.
description: Designed at TypeTogether by Burian and Scaglione, Protipo spans a headline Compact subfamily (Hairline to Black) alongside Narrow, Regular and Wide text cuts, each running seven weights with italics. Subtle details distinguish it from generic grotesques: the 'a' and 'e' shift from single to double-storey in italic, the 'f' gains an italic descender, and diagonal terminals are clipped to maintain tight fit. Variable font support across weight and width axes, plus an accompanying icon set, makes it especially practical for UI, wayfinding and data-heavy editorial work.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Protipo as an information designer's toolbox: a low-contrast sans in three text widths plus a separate headline family, an icon set in two weights, and variable font support. Designed by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione with assistant designer Irene Vlachou, it was built for apps, infographics, UI, wayfinding, transport, posters, and display use, with the aim of helping readers find and process data clearly while still appealing to aesthetics.

name: Rezak
classification_notes: Rezak spans display and text styles but its distinctive identity is driven by incised letterforms inspired by cutting into paper, stone and wood; the Black and Incised display styles define its character, placing it firmly in the display category with a secondary text utility.
summary: A ten-style family by Anya Danilova built around incised letterforms inspired by cutting into paper, stone and wood, spanning bold text weights and a striking trigonometric Incised display style.
description: Designed by Anya Danilova and winner of the 2020 Gerard Unger Scholarship, it separates into text weights (Regular through Extrabold with matching italics) and two display styles, Black and Incised, the latter constructed using trigonometric functions to control its precise geometry. The text styles carry a jaunty energy suited to children's books, editorial subheads and packaging, while the display styles are built for impact in logotypes, posters and screen applications. Coverage extends to Latin and an extended Cyrillic supporting minority languages including Abkhaz, Itelmen and Koryak.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Rezak as a jaunty, dynamic type family that stretches beyond one specific category, inspired by the stout presence of cutting letters from tangible materials. The Black and Incised display styles exploit the relationship between dark and light and what is removed versus what remains, while the four text weights offer bold, energetic performance at smaller sizes. The family supports Latin and extended Cyrillic, with the Incised style built using trigonometric functions for precise control.

name: Rezak Cyrillic
classification_notes: Rezak is inspired by cutting letterforms from physical materials such as paper, stone and wood, with an Incised display style using trigonometric functions; its character shifts between display and text intentions rather than merely changing in weight, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A ten-style Cyrillic display family by Anya Danilova, inspired by cutting letterforms from paper, stone and wood, with an incised display variant and extended Cyrillic support for minority languages including Abkhaz, Itelmen and Koryak.
description: Designed by Anya Danilova and winner of the 2020 Gerard Unger Scholarship as well as a TDC Certificate of Typographic Excellence, Rezak Cyrillic spans ten fonts across five weights with matching italics and a distinctive Incised Black display style built on trigonometric functions. The family changes not only in weight but in intent, moving between a bold, energetic text register and a high-contrast display mode suited to headlines, packaging, branding and logotypes. Its extended Cyrillic coverage reaches minority and endangered languages that most typefaces overlook entirely.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Rezak as a jaunty, dynamic family that refuses to stay within one category or script, inspired by the stout presence of cutting letters from tangible materials. The Black and Incised styles are positioned for impact in headlines, gaming, posters and packaging, while the four text weights with italics suit children's books, movie titles and editorial subheadings across subjects from architecture to cooking.

name: Sakkal Saad
designer: Mamoun Sakkal, Aida Sakkal, Veronika Burian, José Scaglione
classification_notes: A Naskh-style Arabic typeface with handwritten qualities, informed by Middle Eastern newspaper and magazine typography of the 1960s–1980s. The Latin companion is a custom version of Aneto, placing this firmly in the serif/naskh tradition.
summary: A handwritten Naskh revival in spirit, drawn by Mamoun and Aida Sakkal and inspired by the editorial typography of Middle Eastern print culture from the 1960s to 1980s.
description: Designed by Dr. Mamoun Sakkal and Aida Sakkal, with Latin support via a custom version of Aneto by Veronika Burian and José Scaglione, the family spans six weights across text and poster subfamilies for a total of twelve styles. Each style contains over 3,000 glyphs with extensive OpenType features including multiple swash lengths, kashida variants and contextual alternates for Arabic justification. The TDC 2023 Certificate of Typographic Excellence and a Granshan 2023–24 Special Mention reflect its standing in the Arabic type community.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Sakkal Saad as a friendly, handwritten Naskh with a modern feel and a nostalgic vibe, inspired by the energetic typography of Middle Eastern newspapers, magazines and posters from the 1960s to 1980s. Paired with Sakkal Saad Poster, the combined family offers twelve weights with harmonised metrics and a rich OpenType feature set, covering signage, branding, poetry and long-form text settings across digital and print media.

name: Sakkal Saad Poster
classification_notes: A bold, high-contrast Arabic display family in the Naskh tradition, designed explicitly for poster and headline use with chunky, expressive letterforms inspired by 1960s–1980s Middle Eastern print media.
summary: A bold Arabic Naskh display family drawing on the expressive typography of Middle Eastern newspapers and posters from the 1960s to 1980s, awarded a TDC 2023 Certificate of Typographic Excellence.
description: Designed by Dr. Mamoun Sakkal and Aida Sakkal, Sakkal Saad Poster pairs with its text counterpart Sakkal Saad to form a 12-weight system with shared metrics and an extensive OpenType feature set across over 3,000 glyphs per style. Heavier weights increase contrast along the baseline stroke for commanding display use without spreading words unduly across the line. A custom version of the award-winning Aneto Latin, adapted with Arabic-styled dots and punctuation, accompanies the family for cohesive multiscript setting.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Sakkal Saad Poster as a bold, playful, and chunky display family inspired by the energetic typography of Middle Eastern newspapers, magazines, and posters from the 1960s–1980s. Designed by Mamoun and Aida Sakkal, it combines modern typesetting with traditional Naskh aesthetics, and is paired with a custom version of Aneto for multiscript work. The family received a TDC 2023 Certificate of Typographic Excellence and a GRANSHAN 2023–24 Special Mention.

name: Sanserata
classification_notes: Described as an articulated sans with flared terminals, generous x-height and humanist proportions derived from Alverata; the terminal articulation and warm curves place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: Gerard Unger's companion sans to Alverata, built around flared terminals and a generous x-height for readable screen text and cheerful branding.
description: Sanserata extends the concepts behind Unger's Alverata into a sans serif, with articulated terminals that flare towards the end of each stroke to sharpen letterform definition on screen, where rectangular endings tend to blur under emitted light. Seven weights from Light to Black each carry matching italics, and alternate letterforms shift the typeface from a warm, friendly register towards something more businesslike without losing its generous proportions. OpenType features include small caps, tabular and proportional figures, oldstyle figures, ligatures and case-sensitive variants.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Sanserata as a bright and unflappable articulated sans designed by Dr Gerard Unger as a companion to Alverata, suited to positive and casual brands. They note that its articulated terminals, large x-height and short extenders improve readability particularly on screens, and that its two stylistic voices allow it to shift between cheerful and business-casual tones. Production was carried out in collaboration with Tom Grace.

name: Sirba
classification_notes: A low-contrast serif with calligraphic terminals, open counters and curved bracketing; designed for demanding text environments with humanist warmth rather than strict geometric or transitional rigour.
summary: A low-contrast text serif by Nicolien van der Keur, developed from letterpress Bible and dictionary research for demanding small-size settings.
description: Designed with in-depth research into letterpress-printed Bibles and dictionaries, Sirba uses open counters, a large x-height and short ascenders and descenders to maintain legibility at small point sizes. Asymmetrical bottom serifs, curved bracketing and calligraphic terminals give it a warm personality without sacrificing evenness in complex text. Five styles cover Latin (50+ languages), polytonic Greek, a full IPA set and Cyrillic with Bulgarian alternates, alongside small caps, five numeral sets and ornamental glyphs.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Sirba as a friendly, low-contrast serif designed for high-demand environments including dictionaries, academic texts, annual reports, novels and magazines. They note that its design was guided by letterpress Bible and dictionary research, and highlight the Black weight's generous spacing as a deliberate choice to keep counters open at very small sizes. The family's character set covers Latin, polytonic Greek, IPA and Cyrillic, and includes OpenType features such as small caps, extended ligatures, five numeral sets and ornamental symbols.

name: Sirba Cyrillic
classification_notes: Sirba is a low-contrast serif with calligraphic undertones in its terminals and asymmetrical bracketed serifs, designed for sustained reading at small sizes in demanding editorial environments.
summary: A low-contrast serif by Nicolien van der Keur, informed by letterpress Bibles and dictionaries and built for dense, demanding text environments across Latin, Cyrillic and polytonic Greek.
description: Designed with in-depth research into letterpress-printed Bibles and dictionaries, Sirba prioritises legibility at small sizes through open counters, a large x-height and short ascenders and descenders. Calligraphic terminals, asymmetrical bottom serifs and curved bracketing give it warmth without sacrificing evenness across complex settings. The family covers over 50 Latin languages, polytonic Greek, Cyrillic with Bulgarian alternates, and a full IPA symbol set, making it well suited to dictionaries, academic texts, annual reports and multilingual publications.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Sirba as a friendly, low-contrast serif guided by research into letterpress-printed Bibles and dictionaries, designed specifically for demanding text environments such as dictionaries, novels, academic texts and annual reports. The family includes five styles, a full IPA character set, support for Latin, polytonic Greek and Cyrillic with Bulgarian alternates, plus OpenType features including small caps, five numeral sets, ornaments and extended ligatures.

name: Sirba Greek
classification_notes: A low-contrast serif with calligraphic undertones, curved bracketing and asymmetrical serifs, designed for demanding text environments; its open counters and large x-height align it with transitional serif conventions adapted for high legibility.
summary: A low-contrast serif by Nicolien van der Keur, covering polytonic Greek alongside Latin and Cyrillic, designed for demanding small-text environments such as dictionaries and academic publications.
description: Informed by in-depth research into letterpress-printed Bibles and dictionaries, the design features asymmetrical bottom serifs, curved bracketing and calligraphic terminals that give it warmth without sacrificing evenness across complex text. Open counters and a large x-height sustain legibility at very small sizes, while the Black weight is spaced generously to keep counters prominent even at the heaviest grade. The Greek extension was developed in consultation with Irene Vlachou and Gerry Leonidas, and the full character set includes IPA symbols, small caps, five numeral sets and tabular figures consistent across weights.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Sirba Greek as a friendly, low-contrast serif designed specifically for high-demand text environments including dictionaries, academic texts, annual reports, novels and magazines. They highlight its open counters, large x-height and calligraphic details, alongside polytonic Greek support consulted by Irene Vlachou and Gerry Leonidas, a full IPA symbol set, and Cyrillic coverage with Bulgarian alternates.

name: Soleil
classification_notes: Soleil is explicitly described as a geometric sans, though one that deliberately avoids dogmatic geometry in favour of fluid rhythm and hand-sketched warmth. It sits firmly in the geometric sans tradition while borrowing humanist sensibility.
summary: Originally designed for a Viennese labour office wayfinding system, this geometric sans by Wolfgang Homola balances mathematical construction with hand-drawn fluency across seven weights, italics and a display Magic Caps variant.
description: Homola's design process began with extensive hand sketches before digitisation, resulting in a family with generous mirrored counters, tall ascenders, slow curves and a large x-height suited to signage, small text and headlines alike. Seven weights from Light to Black carry matching italics, and a playful MC Escher-inspired Magic Caps style adds a display option for branding and merchandising. A 2026 update brought variable font support on the weight axis alongside Vietnamese and Pinyin coverage, small caps, alternate characters and a full suite of OpenType figure styles.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Soleil as a geometric sans that cleverly avoids being dogmatic about its geometry, combining simplicity with a lively, fluid rhythm born of countless hand sketches. Originally created for a wayfinding and signage system in a 1950s modernist Viennese labour office, the family fits signage, book and magazine design, branding and corporate identity. The Magic Caps variant, inspired by MC Escher, offers an eye-catching option for branding and merchandising.

name: Temeraire
classification_notes: The Regular style is explicitly described as a contrast-loving Transitional Serif with vertical stress; the family also draws on English lettering traditions including Italiennes, copperplates and gravestone cutting, making it a historically informed serif with mixed stylistic registers.
summary: A five-style serif family by Quentin Schmerber drawing on 18th-century English lettering sources, from copperplate scripts and gravestones to Italiennes, designed to be seen rather than ignored.
description: Rather than expanding a family by weight alone, Schmerber researched 18th-century sources to produce five deliberately inconsistent styles, each a homage to a distinct strand of English lettering tradition. The Regular is a high-contrast transitional serif with vertical stress; the Display Black borrows from gravestone cutting with its distinctive looped g and extra-serif t; the Italienne inverts the stress entirely, pushing weight to the horizontal planes for a display style that demands breathing room. Won the 2016 Gerard Unger Scholarship and was selected by the French magazine Étapes for its Diploma Issue the same year.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Temeraire as a typographic exploration into salvaged styles rooted in English lettering tradition, intended for books, magazines and large formats where it can be beheld rather than overlooked. Its five styles range from a contrast-loving transitional regular to an horizontally stressed Italienne, each functioning alongside the others while standing as a standalone homage to specific historical sources.

name: Trevor
classification_notes: Trevor is explicitly described as a slab serif with low contrast and near-monolinear weight distribution, placing it firmly in the slab category. Its narrow proportions, sturdy verticals and ball terminals distinguish it from generic Egyptian slabs.
summary: A narrow, seven-weight slab serif by Teo Tuominen that pairs a sturdy, near-monolinear construction with rounded ball terminals and softened corners.
description: Beginning as a revival of an 18th-century antiqua, Trevor evolved into an original family with a distinctly affable character. Rounded corners throughout mimic ink spread from old metal type, while sparse inktraps and sharp interior angles keep the forms from going soft. Its narrow proportions and high x-height suit headline and intro text, packaging and advertising, and the seven weights offer enough hierarchy for layered editorial work such as playbills and multi-level typesetting. The Black weight is treated as a display cut with adjusted ball terminals and serifs to exploit the additional mass.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Trevor as both heavy and kind, a neighbourly slab serif designed by Teo Tuominen that originated as a revival of an 18th-century antiqua before becoming an entirely original family. They note its near-monolinear weight and high x-height lend a typewriter quality in the lighter cuts, while its sturdy verticality and narrowness suit headlines, packaging and advertising. The seven roman weights cover Latin A Extended and include OpenType features such as proportional and tabular lining and oldstyle figures, ligatures and fractions.

name: Gerard Unger Scholarship
classification_notes: This page describes a scholarship programme rather than a single typeface; no specific typeface category can be determined from the content provided.
summary: An annual scholarship run by TypeTogether that funds, mentors and publishes typeface designs by students and recent graduates, named after the late Dutch type designer Gerard Unger.
description: Launched by TypeTogether and named in honour of the late Dr. Gerard Unger, the scholarship offers selected students a five-month paid internship, a publishing contract with a 50% royalty rate, and mentoring from the TypeTogether team including Veronika Burian and José Scaglione. Previous winners include Roxane Gataud's Bely, Anya Danilova's Rezak and Emma Marichal's Ploquine, with four scholarship typefaces having received TDC awards. A commemorative book, Letters for the Future, marks the programme's tenth anniversary.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe the Gerard Unger Scholarship as an annual programme offering mentorship, publishing and funding to promising typeface design students. Selected designers receive a contract with a 50% royalty rate, retain full ownership of their work, and are paid €5,000 over a five-month internship period, with additional funding of up to €1,000 to attend a top-level typography conference.

name: Premier
classification_notes: The page does not describe a typeface; it describes TypeTogether's Premier membership programme. No typeface classification is possible from the available content.
summary: Premier is TypeTogether's free membership scheme giving designers access to full font families for evaluation before purchase.
description: Rather than a typeface, Premier is TypeTogether's partnership membership, offered free of charge to designers, developers, publishers and agency professionals. It provides access to complete font families so that users can test full ranges before committing to a licence. The programme is framed as a collaborative relationship between the foundry and its clients.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe Premier as a free membership partnership aimed at designers, web developers, publishers and agency folk worldwide, giving them the flexibility to test full font families and make better-informed choices.

name: Self-Hosting Webfonts
classification_notes: This is a catalogue listing page for TypeTogether's entire webfont offering, not a single typeface. No single category or subcategory applies.
summary: A perpetual self-hosting webfont licence scheme covering TypeTogether's full catalogue, with a one-time fee scaled to monthly site traffic.
description: TypeTogether offers perpetual webfont licences for self-hosting via @font-face, delivered in EOT, WOFF, WOFF2 and SVG formats. Pricing is based on expected monthly traffic with no recurring fees or tracking scripts. The catalogue spans over 80 families with extensive script coverage including Arabic, Devanagari, Chinese, Korean and several African and South-East Asian scripts.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describes their webfont self-hosting licence as a one-time fee based on expected monthly site traffic, with no recurring fees, no trackers and no hassle. The full catalogue is optimised for on-screen rendering and available in the most common web font formats. Typographic advice is available for those needing help choosing pairings or suitable screen fonts within a given budget.

name: TypeTogether

summary: An independent Czech type foundry specialising in editorial typefaces and bespoke corporate type design.
description: TypeTogether is based in Prague and focuses on editorial typeface design alongside custom commissions for corporate clients. The foundry offers a retail library as well as bespoke projects. No specific typeface information was available on this contact page.
foundry_blurb: TypeTogether describe themselves as an indie type foundry committed to excellence in type design with a focus on editorial use, also offering custom type design for corporate clients. They invite visitors to browse their retail library or get in touch to discuss custom projects.
